Professor View 01 / 27
Travel English -- 48 Aulas

Victor Malvezi Paschotto

Business English — Corporate Communication

B2 Santo André (SP) Growth Manager — Brink's Online
Progresso Geral0%
Velocidade:
Nome

Victor Malvezi Paschotto

Idade

33 anos

Cidade

Santo André, SP (trabalha em São Paulo)

Profissão

Growth Manager na Brink's (segurança/logística, 50+ países)

Nível

B2 (Upper Intermediate)

Foco

Business English: reuniões, apresentações, dashboards, sotaques

Frequência

1x por semana

Total de Aulas

24 aulas

Personagem-Jornada

De: Um Growth Manager que perde o fio da meada em reuniões multinacionais, omite informações quando não encontra as palavras certas e trava nos verb tenses em contextos corporativos.

Para: Um líder confiante que comunica estratégias, apresenta dashboards e lidera discussões em inglês com stakeholders de 50+ países, com fluência e precisão gramatical. Em 24 aulas.

Promessa Transformadora

De quem "trava e simplifica" para quem "lidera a conversa". Em 24 aulas, Victor vai deixar de omitir informações por falta de vocabulário e passar a comunicar estratégias complexas com confiança em qualquer sala de reunião do mundo. A determinação excepcional dele, combinada com o espanhol fluente, acelera o processo.

Forças
  • Boa compreensão auditiva, entende mais do que produz
  • Determinação excepcional, estuda 7h da manhã antes do trabalho
  • Espanhol fluente (facilita aquisição de vocabulário cognático)
  • Contexto profissional de alta exposição em multinacional global
  • MBA em andamento (terças e quintas) mostra fome de crescimento
Pontos de Melhoria
  • Confusão entre verb tenses (present perfect vs simple past)
  • Uso incorreto de preposições em contextos corporativos
  • Vocabulário corporativo limitado, omite info quando não sabe
  • Dificuldade com diferentes sotaques em reuniões globais
  • Produção oral limitada comparada à compreensão

Currículo Completo — 24 Aulas

#TemaFoco LinguísticoAtividade PrincipalHomework
1Diagnostic + Personal Baseline: Who Is Victor at Brink's?Present perfect vs simple past, reported speech probe, modalsDiagnostic elicitation + 2-min self-intro to the board3-min audio intro + 5 sentences about role using PP/SP
2Running the Numbers: Dashboards and DataDescribing trends, comparatives, superlativesPresent a dashboard and explain KPIs to a VPRecord 3-min dashboard walkthrough
3Leading the Meeting: Agendas and Action ItemsModal verbs (should, could, might), meeting phrasesChair a team meeting with agenda and minutesWrite meeting agenda + action items
4Reporting Up: Status Updates for LeadershipReported speech, indirect questionsGive a status update to senior managementRecord 2-min executive summary
5Emails That Move: Requests, Follow-ups, EscalationsFormal register, hedging, conditionalsWrite and analyze 3 levels of email formalityDraft request + follow-up + escalation emails
6REVIEW: Blocks 1-5 + Mid-block AssessmentConsolidation of all structuresMock board presentation + diagnostic comparisonSelf-assessment journal
7Pitching Ideas: Persuasion and InfluenceSecond conditional, persuasive languagePitch a growth initiative to the C-suitePrepare 5-min pitch + slides outline
8Negotiating: Contracts and AgreementsNegotiation phrases, conditionals, softenersNegotiate a contract with a regional partnerListen to negotiation podcast + notes
9Cross-Cultural Communication: 50 Countries, 50 StylesIndirect language, cultural awareness, register shiftsRole-play with partners from US, India, JapanResearch communication styles of 3 countries
10Handling Difficult ConversationsDiplomatic disagreement, de-escalationNavigate a conflict between stakeholdersRecord diplomatic response scenarios
11Presenting to the Board: Slides and StoriesSignposting, narrative tenses, dramatic presentDeliver a 7-min board presentationPrepare slides + script for a growth report
12REVIEW: Blocks 7-11 + Mock Stakeholder MeetingConsolidationFull stakeholder meeting simulationSelf-assessment journal
13The Accent Challenge: Understanding Global EnglishListening to Indian, British, Australian accentsComprehension drills with varied accentsWatch 3 TED Talks from different accents
14Problem-Solving: When Things Go WrongThird conditional, cause-effect, urgency languageHandle a security operations crisis in EnglishWrite crisis communication email
15Phrasal Verbs That Drive Business15 key business phrasal verbs in contextReplace formal verbs with phrasal verbs in scenariosCreate 10 sentences using new phrasal verbs
16Video Calls and Virtual MeetingsVirtual meeting etiquette, tech vocabularyRun a virtual stakeholder alignment callRecord a virtual presentation
17Small Talk Mastery: Before and After the MeetingConversation starters, follow-up questionsPractice small talk in multiple corporate contextsWatch Simon Sinek on connection + notes
18REVIEW: Blocks 13-17 + Global Meeting SimulationConsolidationMulti-accent meeting simulationSelf-assessment journal
19Feedback and Performance ReviewsReported speech, constructive language, STAR methodConduct a performance review in EnglishWrite 3 STAR-method feedback examples
20Business Idioms and Collocations15 business idioms, collocations with make/do/takeUse idioms in realistic corporate scenariosFind idioms in a business podcast
21Advanced Prepositions: The Details That MatterDependent prepositions, phrasal-prepositional verbsError correction intensive + production drillsComplete preposition exercises + record
22The Growth Pitch: Full Stakeholder PresentationAll structures integrated, persuasion + data + narrativeDeliver a complete growth strategy presentationPrepare and rehearse a 10-min presentation
23Final Diagnostic + Gap AnalysisAll structuresFinal diagnostic + comparison with Aula 1Write personal growth narrative
24Celebration + Continuity PlanConsolidationCelebration + next steps plan for continued growthPersonal English development plan

Metodologia

1. Discovery-Based Grammar

Victor discovers grammar patterns through real corporate examples before seeing the rule. This leverages his strong comprehension and analytical MBA mindset.

2. Stakeholder-Driven Content

Every lesson uses Brink's context: board presentations, KPI discussions, cross-regional meetings. Victor practices what he will actually do at work.

3. Multi-Accent Exposure

Progressive exposure to American, British, Indian, and Australian accents. Brink's operates in 50+ countries, so accent flexibility is critical.

4. Production Over Comprehension

Victor understands more than he produces. Every lesson pushes production: role-plays, presentations, elevator pitches. Target: close the comprehension-production gap.

5. Spanish as Accelerator

Leverage Victor's fluent Spanish for cognate vocabulary (strategy/estrategia, performance/performance). Monitor for false friends and interference patterns.

Mapa de Personalidade

IntrovertidoExtrovertido
Aprendiz visualAprendiz auditivo
DetalhistaBig picture
Preferência por regrasPreferência por prática
Ritmo lentoRitmo acelerado
Evita riscosAceita desafios
Foco acadêmicoFoco profissional
Aula 01 -- Pre-class

Diagnostic + Personal Baseline

Who Is Victor Paschotto at Brink's? Key words: growth, strategy, target, stakeholder, KPI, deliverable, oversee, carry out, make headway, performance. Structures: present perfect vs simple past in professional narratives.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Growth -- an increase in size, value, or importance over time
"We have achieved consistent growth in the Latin American market."
Strategy -- a plan of action designed to achieve a long-term goal
"Our expansion strategy focuses on emerging markets."
Target -- a specific result or goal that you aim to achieve
"We exceeded our revenue target by fifteen percent last quarter."
Stakeholder -- a person or group with an interest in an organization
"All stakeholders were informed about the new compliance policy."
KPI -- Key Performance Indicator: a measurable value showing progress
"Customer retention is one of our most important KPIs."
Deliverable -- a tangible output that must be produced to complete a project
"The main deliverable for this sprint is the market analysis report."
Oversee -- to watch over and direct work, a project, or people
"I oversee growth initiatives across five regional markets."
Carry out -- to perform, execute, or complete a task or plan
"The team carried out the pilot program in three cities."
Make headway -- to make progress, especially when it is difficult
"We have made headway on the new security platform despite challenges."
Performance -- how well a person, team, or company does their work
"The team's performance this quarter has been outstanding."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Growth
Strategy
Target
Stakeholder
KPI
Deliverable
Oversee
Carry out
Make headway
Performance

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Marcus joined a multinational security company as a Growth Manager two years ago. Since then, he has overseen multiple expansion projects and has made headway on entering new markets. His role involves developing strategies to meet ambitious targets and working closely with stakeholders across the region. Last quarter, his team exceeded the revenue target by ten percent, which was a major achievement. "I have carried out three pilot programs this year," he explains. "The key deliverables are always tied to clear KPIs, and that keeps our performance on track." Before this role, Marcus worked at a consulting firm where he gained experience in corporate strategy.

1. Why does Marcus say "I have carried out three pilot programs this year" using the present perfect?
A Because the pilot programs happened at a specific time last month.
B Because the experience started in the past and the year is still ongoing.
C Because he is carrying out a program right now.
2. Why is "Last quarter, his team exceeded the target" in the simple past?
A Because "last quarter" is a finished, specific period in the past.
B Because the team is still exceeding the target now.
C Because simple past is used for future plans.
3. Which sentence shows an action that started in the past and continues to the present?
A "Marcus joined the company two years ago."
B "Before this role, Marcus worked at a consulting firm."
C "He has overseen multiple expansion projects since then."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Perfect vs Simple Past

GRAMMAR

When to use each tense in professional contexts.

TenseUseExample
Simple PastCompleted action at a specific time in the pastI joined Brink's in 2022.
Signal words (Past)yesterday, last week/month/year, in 2022, agoWe exceeded the target last quarter.
Present PerfectExperience connected to the present, ongoing resultI have overseen multiple projects.
Signal words (Perfect)since, for, already, recently, so far, this yearWe have made headway since January.
Key differenceSimple past = WHEN it happened matters. Present perfect = the RESULT or EXPERIENCE matters, not when.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We have achieved consistent in the LATAM market."
"I growth initiatives across five regional markets."
"The team the pilot program in three cities."
"Customer retention is one of our most important ."
"We have made significant on the new platform."
"The main for this sprint is the market analysis."

Stage 2: Put the Meeting in Order

Order

Put the steps of a professional introduction at a corporate meeting in the correct order.

?Explain your main responsibilities and KPIs.
?Close by expressing interest in future collaboration.
?Greet the person and state your name.
?Mention a recent achievement or project.
?State your title and the company you work for.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I am responsible for growth initiatives in the LATAM region.
My role involves developing strategies and tracking KPIs.
I work closely with stakeholders across different markets.
We have made headway on several growth projects since January.
The team carried out the pilot program and exceeded the target.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

A VP from headquarters asks you to describe your role at Brink's. What do you say?
A "I do growth things at the company."
B "I am responsible for growth initiatives in the LATAM region. I oversee strategy development and work closely with stakeholders."
C "I make numbers go up at Brink's."
Someone asks: "How long have you been at Brink's?" You respond:
A "I am at Brink's since 2022."
B "I have been with Brink's since 2022. I joined as a Growth Manager and have overseen several expansion projects."
C "I worked at Brink's for a long time."
Your director asks about the progress on a project. The most professional response is:
A "We are doing okay with the project."
B "The project is fine, no problems."
C "We have made significant headway on the project. The key deliverables are on track, and our KPIs show a positive trend."
An international colleague asks about a recent achievement. You say:
A "Last quarter, we exceeded our revenue target by fifteen percent. We carried out three expansion initiatives in the region."
B "We did good things last time."
C "Our results have been last quarter very impressive."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are at Brink's global leadership summit. A senior executive from the US headquarters walks up to you. Introduce yourself: your name, your title, your main responsibilities, one recent achievement, and what you are working on now. Use present perfect and simple past. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 1

1I am responsible for growth initiatives in the LATAM region.
2My role involves developing strategies and tracking KPIs.
3I work closely with stakeholders across different markets.
4We have made headway on several growth projects.
5It was great meeting you. I look forward to collaborating.
Aula 02 -- Pre-class

Owning the Room -- Presenting Growth Data

Presenting growth data to international stakeholders. Key words: outperform, benchmark, conversion rate, pipeline, traction, headcount, revenue stream, forecast, variance, alignment. Structure: third conditional for post-performance analysis.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Outperform -- to do better than a competitor or standard
"Our LATAM division outperformed all other regions in Q3."
Benchmark -- a standard or reference point for comparison
"We use industry benchmarks to measure our progress."
Conversion rate -- the percentage of leads that become customers
"Our conversion rate improved from 2.8% to 3.2% after the campaign."
Pipeline -- the total value of potential deals in progress
"We have a strong pipeline of $2.4 million for Q4."
Traction -- progress or momentum in growing a business
"The new product is gaining traction in the Brazilian market."
Headcount -- the total number of employees in a team or company
"We need to increase headcount by five to support the expansion."
Revenue stream -- a distinct source of income for a company
"Our main revenue stream comes from enterprise contracts."
Forecast -- a prediction of future performance or results
"The forecast for Q4 suggests a twelve percent revenue increase."
Variance -- the difference between expected and actual results
"The variance between forecast and actuals was due to the delayed campaign."
Alignment -- agreement among people toward a common goal
"We need alignment from all regional leads before the board meeting."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Outperform
Benchmark
Conversion rate
Pipeline
Traction
Headcount
Revenue stream
Forecast
Variance
Alignment

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

After Q3 results were released, the Growth Manager reviewed the pipeline and revenue stream data for the LATAM region. The conversion rate had dropped to 3.2%, below the benchmark of 4%. He analyzed the variance and concluded that the delayed campaign launch was the main factor. If the team had launched the campaign two weeks earlier, they would have hit the original target. The region's pipeline still grew by 32%, outperforming other divisions, but the headcount reduction had slowed progress. If they had not lost two team members, they would have outperformed every benchmark. The Growth Manager prepared a forecast for Q4, predicting a fifteen percent increase in revenue. He needed to make a compelling case to the VP Americas that LATAM was gaining traction and deserved additional investment. "These results are just the tip of the iceberg," he explained. If we had had the full team, we would have reached the five million milestone."

1. Why does the text say "If the team had launched the campaign earlier, they would have hit the target"?
A Because the team launched the campaign on time and hit the target.
B Because the team did NOT launch on time, and this is an imaginary past result.
C Because the team will launch the campaign in the future.
2. What is the function of the third conditional in business analysis?
A To describe actions that are happening right now.
B To make predictions about the future.
C To analyze what could have been different in the past and its imaginary result.
3. In the sentence "If they had not lost two team members, they would have outperformed every benchmark," what does the negative form indicate?
A They DID lose two members, and the sentence imagines the opposite scenario.
B They did not lose any members and everything went well.
C They are planning to hire two new members next quarter.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Third Conditional

GRAMMAR

How to talk about imaginary past situations and their imaginary results.

PartStructureExample
IF clauseIf + subject + had + past participleIf we had launched earlier...
Result clausesubject + would have + past participle...we would have hit the target.
Negative IFIf + subject + had not + past participleIf we had not lost headcount...
Negative resultsubject + would not have + past participle...we would not have missed the target.
Key pointThe third conditional talks about the PAST. The situation is IMAGINARY. It did NOT happen. WOULD goes in the result, NEVER in the IF clause.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word or phrase. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Our division all other regions in Q3."
"If we had launched the campaign on time, we the conversion rate target."
"The for Q4 suggests a twelve percent revenue increase."
"We need from all regional leads before the board meeting."
"The new product is gaining in the Brazilian market."
"The between forecast and actuals was primarily due to the delayed launch."

Stage 2: Put the Presentation in Order

Order

Put the steps of a data presentation in the correct sequence.

?Explain the variance between forecast and actual results.
?Present your recommendation and forecast for next quarter.
?Open with the headline: the key metric that stands out.
?Use the third conditional to analyze what could have been different.
?Walk through the main KPIs: pipeline, conversion rate, revenue.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

If we had launched the campaign earlier, we would have hit the Q3 target.
Our pipeline grew by thirty-two percent, outperforming every benchmark.
Roughly speaking, the forecast shows a fifteen percent increase for Q4.
I need to make a compelling case for increasing our headcount.
These results are just the tip of the iceberg. We need to address concerns and implement a strategy.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

A VP asks why the conversion rate was below the benchmark. What do you say?
A "The numbers were bad because of problems."
B "The variance was primarily due to a delayed campaign launch. If we had launched on schedule, we would have hit the 4% benchmark."
C "It is not our fault. The market was difficult."
You need to present the pipeline growth diplomatically. What do you say?
A "The pipeline is definitely going to double next quarter."
B "I think the pipeline might maybe possibly grow."
C "Roughly speaking, the pipeline has grown by thirty-two percent, and it would appear that this trend will continue into Q4."
A director asks: "What would have happened if you had not lost team members?" How do you respond?
A "If we had not lost two team members, we would have outperformed every benchmark in the region."
B "If we would not have lost team members, we would have outperformed."
C "We did not lose members, so everything was fine."
You want to argue for more investment in LATAM. Which is the most professional approach?
A "We need more money. The other regions get more than us."
B "I would like to make a compelling case for additional investment. LATAM is gaining traction, and the forecast shows we could reach a revenue milestone of five million."
C "Give us more headcount and we will be fine."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are presenting the Q3 results to Brink's international leadership. Walk them through: the pipeline growth, the conversion rate variance, what could have been different (use the third conditional), and your forecast for Q4. Use hedging language where appropriate. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 2

1If we had launched the campaign earlier, we would have hit the Q3 target.
2Our pipeline grew by thirty-two percent, outperforming every benchmark.
3Roughly speaking, the forecast shows a fifteen percent increase for Q4.
4I need to make a compelling case for increasing our headcount.
5These results are just the tip of the iceberg.
Aula 03 -- Pre-class

Navigating Tough Conversations -- Pushback, Objections, and Diplomatic Disagreement

Handling pushback, objections, and diplomatic disagreement. Key words: pushback, friction, escalate, mediate, counterpart, resolution, grievance, mandate, leverage, navigate. Structure: modal perfects (should have, must have, might have).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Pushback -- resistance or opposition to a plan or idea
"We received significant pushback from HQ about the new pricing strategy."
Friction -- tension or disagreement between people or groups
"There has been some friction between the LATAM and APAC teams over resource allocation."
Escalate -- to make a problem more serious or bring it to higher authority
"If we cannot resolve this internally, I will need to escalate the issue to the VP."
Mediate -- to help two sides reach an agreement
"The regional director had to mediate between the two teams to find a compromise."
Counterpart -- a person holding a similar position in another organization or region
"I need to align with my counterpart in London before we finalize the proposal."
Resolution -- the act of solving a problem or settling a disagreement
"We reached a resolution that satisfied both the LATAM and EMEA teams."
Grievance -- a formal complaint about unfair treatment
"The employee filed a grievance about the decision to relocate the team."
Mandate -- an official order or requirement from an authority
"The London office issued a mandate to cut regional marketing budgets by twenty percent."
Leverage -- to use something strategically to gain an advantage
"We can leverage our Q3 results to negotiate a bigger budget for next year."
Navigate -- to find a way through a difficult or complex situation
"As Growth Manager, Victor has to navigate competing priorities from multiple regions."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Pushback
Friction
Escalate
Mediate
Counterpart
Resolution
Grievance
Mandate
Leverage
Navigate

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

After the Q1 strategy meeting, the Growth Manager reviewed the feedback from all regional directors. There had been significant pushback from the APAC team, and the friction between regions was escalating. The London office had issued a mandate to cut marketing budgets by twenty percent, but the Growth Manager believed the board should have consulted the regional leads before making that decision. The delay in communication must have caused the misalignment, because none of the directors had the same information. The APAC counterpart might have misunderstood the scope of the LATAM proposal, which led to further friction. To reach a resolution, the Growth Manager decided to put forward a proposal that took into consideration both regional priorities. He planned to leverage the strong pipeline data to navigate the board's concerns. "We could have avoided this entire conflict if we had communicated earlier," he noted. The employee who had filed a grievance about the budget decision was invited to the meeting as well. Reading between the lines, the Growth Manager understood that the board was open to compromise, but only if the regions presented a unified front.

1. Why does the text say the board "should have consulted" the regional leads?
A Because the board did consult them and everyone agreed.
B Because the board did NOT consult them, and the writer believes they should have.
C Because the board will consult them in the future.
2. What does "must have caused" express in this context?
A A prediction about the future.
B A strong belief that something happened in the past based on evidence.
C An obligation that was not fulfilled.
3. In the sentence "The APAC counterpart might have misunderstood the scope," what level of certainty does "might have" express?
A The writer is 100% certain the counterpart misunderstood.
B The writer knows for a fact nothing was misunderstood.
C The writer thinks it is possible but not certain that the counterpart misunderstood.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Modal Perfects

GRAMMAR

How to express judgments, certainty, and speculation about past events.

ModalStructureMeaningExample
should haveshould + have + past participleCriticism or regret: it was the right thing but did NOT happen"They should have aligned the team."
must havemust + have + past participleHigh certainty (90%+): I am almost sure this happened"The delay must have affected Q4."
might havemight + have + past participleLow certainty (40-60%): it is possible but uncertain"The client might have misunderstood."
could havecould + have + past participleMissed opportunity: it was possible but did not happen"We could have avoided the friction."
Key rule: After modal + HAVE, always use the PAST PARTICIPLE. Never add "to" (should have to align) and never use the base form (must have affect).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word or phrase. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We received significant from HQ about the new pricing strategy."
"They the team before the product launch."
"The delay Q4 results."
"We can our Q3 results to negotiate a bigger budget."
"I would like to that takes into consideration both priorities."
"A unified front is our best path to ."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Put the steps of handling a professional disagreement in the correct sequence.

?Present your counter-proposal with supporting data.
?Confirm the resolution and next steps in writing.
?Acknowledge the other person's concern diplomatically.
?Negotiate a compromise that addresses both sides.
?Identify the root cause of the disagreement.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I take your point, however, the data shows a different picture.
They should have aligned the team before the product launch.
I would like to put forward a proposal that takes into consideration both priorities.
We can leverage the data to navigate the board's concerns.
With respect, the data shows a different picture. A unified front is our best path to resolution.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

A colleague disagrees with your strategy in a meeting. What is the most diplomatic response?
A "You are wrong. The numbers prove it."
B "I take your point, however, the data shows a different picture. One could argue that our current trajectory supports the plan."
C "Fine, let us just do it your way then."
You believe the board made a mistake by not consulting regional directors. How do you express this?
A "The board was stupid not to ask us first."
B "The board will consult us next time."
C "The board should have consulted the regional directors before issuing the mandate."
You think the client probably received wrong information, but you are not sure. What do you say?
A "The client might have received the wrong information from our counterpart."
B "The client definitely received the wrong information."
C "The client should have to receive better information."
You want to formally suggest a joint strategy to the VP. What is the most professional approach?
A "I have an idea. Just do what I say."
B "I would like to put forward a proposal that takes into consideration both regional priorities and leverages our strong pipeline data."
C "Someone should fix this problem soon."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are in a meeting with a senior director who disagrees with your Q1 growth strategy. Walk them through: acknowledge their concern, explain what should have been done differently (use modal perfects), present your counter-proposal using diplomatic language, and explain how you plan to navigate the situation. Use hedging language and at least three vocabulary words from this lesson. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 3

1I take your point, however, the data shows a different picture.
2They should have aligned the team before the launch.
3I would like to put forward a proposal that takes into consideration both priorities.
4We can leverage the data to navigate the board's concerns.
5A unified front is our best path to resolution.
Aula 04 -- Pre-class

Reporting What Was Said -- Meetings, Briefings, and Follow-Up Communication

Reporting speech from meetings and briefings. Key words: briefing, debrief, circulate, confirm, clarify, proceed, outline, commit, consensus, directive. Structure: reported speech (tense backshifts, pronoun/time shifts).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Briefing -- a short meeting to share key information or updates
"The morning briefing covered the new security protocols for all LATAM offices."
Debrief -- a session to review what happened after an event or meeting
"After the leadership call, Victor scheduled a debrief with the LATAM team."
Circulate -- to distribute a document or information to a group
"Please circulate the meeting minutes to all regional directors by end of day."
Confirm -- to verify that something is true, agreed, or final
"The CFO confirmed that the budget cuts would take effect in Q2."
Clarify -- to make something clearer or easier to understand
"I need to clarify what the director meant about the timeline changes."
Proceed -- to continue or move forward with a plan or action
"The VP said we should proceed with the original timeline despite the setbacks."
Outline -- to describe the main points or structure of something
"Let me outline the three key decisions from the leadership meeting."
Commit -- to pledge or dedicate yourself to a task or decision
"The regional directors committed to delivering the revised targets by Friday."
Consensus -- a general agreement reached by a group
"After two hours of discussion, the team finally reached a consensus on the new strategy."
Directive -- an official instruction or order from a higher authority
"The new directive from London requires all regions to reduce operational costs by ten percent."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Briefing
Debrief
Circulate
Confirm
Clarify
Proceed
Outline
Commit
Consensus
Directive

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

After the quarterly strategy meeting, the Growth Manager sat down to write a follow-up email to Elena Torres in Buenos Aires. He needed to debrief her on everything that had been discussed. The CFO had confirmed that the company was cutting the LATAM marketing budget by fifteen percent. She mentioned that this was a global directive from London, not a regional decision. The VP of Operations told the team he had already submitted the headcount report, but he warned that the board would not approve any new hires that quarter. The regional director suggested they might revisit the growth targets if Q2 numbers improved. She acknowledged the current goals were ambitious but insisted the team should proceed with the original plan. The Growth Manager decided to outline the three key decisions and circulate the notes before the end of the day. He knew the team needed to reach a consensus on next steps quickly. Everyone had committed to delivering revised targets by Friday, so there was no time to waste. He wrote: "Let me cut to the chase: the briefing confirmed three things." He hoped this level of clarity would bring about change in how the regions communicated directives.

1. Why did the Growth Manager need to write a follow-up email?
A Because he wanted to cancel the next meeting.
B Because he needed to debrief Elena on what had been discussed at the strategy meeting.
C Because Elena asked him to write a new strategy proposal.
2. In the sentence "The CFO had confirmed that the company was cutting the budget," why does the text use "was cutting" instead of "is cutting"?
A Because the budget cut was already completed.
B Because the writer is reporting what was said in the past, so the tense shifts back (present continuous becomes past continuous).
C Because the CFO was unsure about the decision.
3. What does the expression "cut to the chase" mean in this context?
A To reduce the number of meetings.
B To avoid sharing bad news.
C To get directly to the main point without unnecessary detail.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Reported Speech

GRAMMAR

How to report what someone said: tense backshifts, pronoun changes, and time shifts.

Direct SpeechReported SpeechExample
Present Simple (am/is/are)Past Simple (was/were)"I work here" → He said he worked there
Present ContinuousPast Continuous"We are cutting" → She said they were cutting
Present PerfectPast Perfect"I have submitted" → He said he had submitted
willwould"It will be delayed" → She said it would be delayed
cancould"We can proceed" → He said they could proceed
might / could / wouldno change"We might revisit" → They said they might revisit
Key rules: SAY + (that) clause. TELL + person + (that) clause. Never "said me" or "told that." Pronouns shift: I → he/she, we → they. Time words shift: today → that day, tomorrow → the next day, yesterday → the day before.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word or phrase. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"The CFO that the budget would be cut by fifteen percent."
"He told me he the headcount report."
"She said the launch be delayed until Q3."
"Please the meeting notes to all regional directors."
"They finally managed to on the new strategy."
"The director that the team should proceed with the original plan."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Put the steps of writing a professional follow-up email in the correct sequence.

?Report what each speaker said using reported speech.
?Close the email with a call to action and deadline.
?State the purpose of the email and which meeting you are reporting on.
?Highlight any unresolved items that need clarification.
?Outline the key decisions and action items from the meeting.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

She confirmed that the budget would be cut by fifteen percent.
He told me he had already submitted the headcount report.
They mentioned they might revisit the targets if Q2 numbers improved.
Let me outline the key decisions and we can proceed from there.
I will circulate the meeting notes so we can reach a consensus on next steps.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

A colleague asks what the CFO said in the meeting. She originally said: "We are cutting the budget next quarter." How do you report this?
A "The CFO said we are cutting the budget next quarter."
B "The CFO said they were cutting the budget the following quarter."
C "The CFO told that they cut the budget."
Your manager originally said: "I will circulate the notes tomorrow." You are reporting this the next week. What do you say?
A "He said he will circulate the notes tomorrow."
B "He said me he would circulate the notes."
C "He said he would circulate the notes the next day."
You want to quickly summarize the main points of a meeting for a colleague. Which expression is most appropriate?
A "Let me tell you everything from the beginning, step by step."
B "Let me cut to the chase: here are the three key decisions."
C "I do not remember much, but it was a long meeting."
The director said something negative: "We did NOT approve the new hires." Which reporting verb best captures the tone?
A "The director mentioned the new hires."
B "The director warned that the board would not approve any new hires."
C "The director suggested new hires."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you attended a strategy meeting at Brinks where three things were decided: budget cuts, a hiring freeze, and revised growth targets. Your colleague Elena missed the meeting. Record a 2-minute verbal debrief for her. Report what each person said using reported speech, include at least 3 reporting verbs (mentioned, confirmed, warned, suggested, insisted, acknowledged), and use vocabulary from this lesson (briefing, debrief, circulate, outline, consensus, directive). End by telling Elena what the next steps are.

Survival Card -- Lesson 4

1She confirmed that the budget would be cut by fifteen percent.
2He told me he had already submitted the report.
3They mentioned they might revisit the targets next quarter.
4I will circulate the meeting notes so we can reach a consensus.
5Let me outline the key decisions and we can proceed from there.
Aula 05 -- Pre-class

Emails That Move -- Requests, Follow-Ups, and Escalations

Writing professional emails that get results. Key words: recipient, concise, prompt, discrepancy, reiterate, pertinent, courtesy, liaise, outstanding, tactful. Structure: formal register, hedging, and conditionals for polite requests and escalations.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Recipient -- the person who receives an email, message, or document
"Always check that the recipient is correct before you click send on a sensitive email."
Concise -- giving a lot of information clearly and in a few words
"Keep the update concise so the directors can read it in under a minute."
Prompt -- done quickly, without delay
"Thank you for your prompt reply; it helped us close the order on time."
Discrepancy -- a difference or inconsistency between two things that should match
"There is a discrepancy between the invoice and the quantity we actually received."
Reiterate -- to say or write something again to emphasize it
"Let me reiterate that the deadline for the LATAM report is this Friday."
Pertinent -- directly relevant and connected to the matter being discussed
"Please include only the pertinent figures so the message stays focused."
Courtesy -- polite behavior, or a polite and considerate action
"As a courtesy, she copied the regional team on the supplier update."
Liaise -- to communicate and coordinate with others to work together effectively
"Victor will liaise with the London office to align the security rollout."
Outstanding -- not yet resolved, completed, or paid
"There are two outstanding items from last week that still need a response."
Tactful -- careful not to offend or upset others; diplomatic in wording
"A tactful email asks for action without sounding like a demand."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Recipient
Concise
Prompt
Discrepancy
Reiterate
Pertinent
Courtesy
Liaise
Outstanding
Tactful

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The Growth Manager opened his inbox and saw a problem. A supplier had missed a deadline, and there was a clear discrepancy between the order and the delivery. His first instinct was to write something direct: "Fix this now." But he knew a tactful message would get a better result. Instead of a demand, he chose a hedged, conditional request. He wrote: "I would appreciate it if you could confirm the corrected quantity by Thursday." He kept the email concise and included only the pertinent details, so the recipient could act quickly. As a courtesy, he copied his counterpart in London so she could liaise with the supplier directly. He also decided to reiterate the original requirements, because they had been agreed weeks earlier. At the end, he added a soft escalation: "If we do not receive the figures by Friday, we will raise the matter with the regional director." It was firm but polite. He asked for a prompt reply at the recipient's earliest convenience, and he closed the message warmly. Two outstanding issues remained, but the tone of the email made it far more likely that they would be resolved without friction.

1. Why did the Growth Manager NOT write "Fix this now"?
A Because he did not know who the recipient was.
B Because a tactful, hedged request gets a better result than a blunt demand.
C Because the problem was not important enough to mention.
2. What is the function of the sentence "If we do not receive the figures by Friday, we will raise the matter with the regional director"?
A It is an apology for the delay.
B It is a polite but firm escalation, using a first conditional to state the consequence.
C It is a question asking for permission.
3. Why did he include only the "pertinent" details and keep the email "concise"?
A So the email would look longer and more serious.
B Because he did not have time to write more.
C So the recipient could understand the issue and act on it quickly.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Register, Hedging & Conditional Requests

GRAMMAR

The same request can sound rude or professional depending on the register. Hedging softens it; conditionals make it diplomatic.

RegisterStructureExample
Direct (avoid)Imperative"Send me the report."
NeutralCan / Could you...?"Could you send me the report?"
Formal (hedged)I would appreciate it if you could..."I would appreciate it if you could send me the report."
TentativeI was wondering if..."I was wondering if you could send the report today."
EscalationFirst conditional: If + present, will + base"If we do not receive it by Friday, we will escalate."
Hedging devices: would / could / might / I was wondering if / it would be helpful if / perhaps / at your earliest convenience. Rule: the more important or sensitive the request, the more you hedge. Use "would appreciate it if you could + base verb" (NOT "to send"). PT: quanto mais delicado o pedido, mais voce suaviza -- evite o imperativo direto.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word or phrase. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I confirm the figures by Thursday."
"There is a between the invoice and the delivery."
" we do not receive the files by Friday, we will escalate the matter."
"Thank you for your reply on the supplier issue."
"Please with the London office before you confirm the deadline."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Put the steps of writing a professional escalation email in the correct sequence.

?Make a tactful, hedged request for the specific action you need.
?Close with a courteous line and a request for a prompt reply.
?Open with a clear subject line and a polite greeting to the recipient.
?Add a soft escalation using a first conditional if the issue is urgent.
?State the issue concisely, including only the pertinent facts.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I would appreciate it if you could send me the report by Thursday.
I am writing to follow up on my previous email about the outstanding order.
If we do not receive a reply by Friday, we will have to escalate the matter.
I was wondering if you could clarify the discrepancy in the figures.
Thank you for your prompt reply; I look forward to hearing from you.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

You need a colleague to send a file urgently, but you want to stay polite and professional. What do you write?
A "Send me the file now."
B "I would appreciate it if you could send me the file by the end of the day."
C "Why have you not sent the file yet?"
A supplier has ignored two emails and the issue is now urgent. How do you escalate tactfully?
A "You never answer. This is unacceptable."
B "If we do not receive a response by Friday, we will have to escalate the matter to the regional director."
C "I will report you to my boss today."
You are writing a follow-up email after a week of silence. How do you open?
A "I am writing to follow up on my previous email regarding the outstanding order."
B "Did you forget about me again?"
C "Just checking if you are still alive."
Your director asks why your email to a partner was so effective. What is the best explanation?
A "It was long and included every detail of the project."
B "It was concise and tactful, included only the pertinent facts, and made a clear hedged request."
C "It was a direct order, so they had no choice."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine a supplier at Brinks is two weeks late delivering security hardware, and there is a discrepancy between the order and the delivery. Record a spoken version of a follow-up and escalation email. Open politely with the recipient in mind, state the issue concisely using only pertinent facts, make a tactful hedged request ("I would appreciate it if you could..."), add a soft escalation with a first conditional ("If we do not... we will..."), and close by asking for a prompt reply. Use at least 4 words from this lesson.

Survival Card -- Lesson 5

1I would appreciate it if you could send me the report by Thursday.
2I am writing to follow up on my previous email about the outstanding order.
3If we do not receive a reply by Friday, we will have to escalate the matter.
4I was wondering if you could clarify the discrepancy in the figures.
5Thank you for your prompt reply; I look forward to hearing from you.
Aula 06 -- Pre-class

The Mid-Block Review -- Putting Lessons 1 to 5 Together

A checkpoint that consolidates the whole block. Review words: stakeholder, oversee, benchmark, forecast, leverage, escalate, circulate, consensus, liaise, concise. Five structures side by side: present perfect, third conditional, modal perfects, reported speech, and formal register.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

A review of the key terms from Lessons 1 to 5. Tap Listen to refresh each one.

Stakeholder -- a person or group with an interest in a project or its outcome
"Every major stakeholder has signed off on the new security platform."
Oversee -- to watch over and be responsible for a process or team
"I oversee growth initiatives across five regional offices."
Benchmark -- a standard or point of reference used to measure performance
"Our division outperformed the industry benchmark this quarter."
Forecast -- a prediction of future results, numbers, or conditions
"The forecast for Q4 suggests a twelve percent revenue increase."
Leverage -- to use a resource or position to gain the greatest advantage
"We can leverage our Q3 results to negotiate a bigger budget."
Escalate -- to raise an issue to a higher level of authority for action
"If the supplier goes quiet, we will escalate the matter to the director."
Circulate -- to send a document or message around to a group of people
"I will circulate the meeting notes to all regional leads."
Consensus -- a general agreement reached by a group after discussion
"The team finally reached a consensus on the new targets."
Liaise -- to communicate and coordinate with others to work together
"Victor will liaise with the London office to align the rollout."
Concise -- giving a lot of information clearly and in a few words
"Keep the review concise so the board can act quickly."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Stakeholder
Oversee
Benchmark
Forecast
Leverage
Escalate
Circulate
Consensus
Liaise
Concise

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the review summary and answer the questions.

The Growth Manager prepared his mid-block review. He opened with the wins: the team had outperformed its benchmark and had made real headway on the security platform, and the pipeline had grown well above forecast. He was honest about the Q2 slip too. If they had aligned the regional leads earlier, they would have avoided the friction over headcount. He speculated carefully about the cause: the delay must have cost a few weeks, and the client might have misunderstood the scope. Then he debriefed the leadership call. The director had confirmed that the budget would hold, and she mentioned they might revisit the targets. Finally, he closed with the right register: "I would appreciate it if you could review these figures, and I will circulate a concise summary at your earliest convenience." He knew that one report, built from five tools, would do the work of ten emails.

1. Which structure does the manager use to present the team's wins?
A The present perfect / past perfect, to show results achieved up to that point.
B The first conditional, to describe a future risk.
C The imperative, to give the board an order.
2. What does "If they had aligned the regional leads earlier, they would have avoided the friction" express?
A A real plan for next quarter.
B A third conditional: a past result that did not happen.
C A polite request for help.
3. Why does he say "the delay must have cost a few weeks" rather than "the delay cost a few weeks"?
A Because he is certain and has the exact figures.
B Because a modal perfect signals a careful, diplomatic guess about the past.
C Because it is a direct quotation from the director.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Five-Tool Cheat Sheet

GRAMMAR

One job, one tool. This table maps each function from the block to its structure.

JobStructureExample
Achievement (L1)have / has + past participle"We have made headway."
Unreal past (L2)If + had + p.p., would have + p.p."If we had launched earlier, we would have won."
Speculation (L3)must / might / could + have + p.p."The delay must have affected Q4."
Debrief (L4)reported speech (backshift)"She said the budget would be cut."
Register (L5)I would appreciate it if you could + base"I would appreciate it if you could confirm."
Watch the traps: present perfect never goes with a finished time word (use past simple); in a third conditional the if-clause is "had + p.p.", never "would have"; modal perfects always keep "have". PT: cada situacao tem UMA ferramenta -- conquista usa present perfect, passado irreal usa third conditional, suposicao usa modal perfect, relato usa discurso indireto, e pedido usa registro formal.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Since January, the team its benchmark."
"If we the leads earlier, we would have avoided the slip."
"The board noticed the variance in the figures."
"She confirmed that the budget be cut by fifteen percent."
"I would appreciate it if you could the summary by Friday."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Put the steps of delivering a strong mid-block review in the correct sequence.

?Speculate diplomatically about the cause using a modal perfect.
?Close with a tactful, hedged request for the next step.
?Open with the achievements using the present perfect.
?Debrief the board's decision using reported speech.
?Own a setback honestly using a third conditional.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

We have outperformed the benchmark and made real headway this quarter.
If we had aligned the leads earlier, we would have avoided the slip.
The delay must have affected Q4, but the team navigated it well.
She confirmed the budget would hold and mentioned they might revisit the targets.
I would appreciate it if you could review the figures at your earliest convenience.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each review situation.

You want to highlight a result the team achieved this year, with no specific finished date. Which is correct?
A "We have reached the milestone last quarter."
B "We have reached an important milestone this year."
C "We are reaching the milestone two years ago."
The board asks why the Q2 launch underperformed. How do you explain the missed result diplomatically?
A "If we had aligned the team earlier, we would have hit the target."
B "If we would align the team, we will hit the target."
C "If we align the team earlier, we hit the target."
You are reporting what the director told you in a private call. Which sentence is correct?
A "She said the budget will be cut and they will revisit the targets."
B "She said the budget would be cut and they might revisit the targets."
C "She say the budget would be cut and revisit the targets."
You want to close your review by asking the board to send feedback, without sounding demanding. What do you say?
A "Send me your feedback now."
B "I would appreciate it if you could share your feedback at your earliest convenience."
C "Why have you not sent feedback yet?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Record a ninety-second mid-block review of your real work at Brinks this quarter. Open with achievements (present perfect), explain one setback honestly (third conditional), speculate about its cause (a modal perfect), report a decision someone shared with you (reported speech), and close with a tactful next step (a hedged request). Use all five tools and at least four words from the block.

Survival Card -- Lesson 6

1We have outperformed the benchmark and made real headway this quarter.
2If we had aligned the leads earlier, we would have avoided the slip.
3The delay must have affected Q4, but the team navigated it well.
4She confirmed the budget would hold and mentioned they might revisit the targets.
5I would appreciate it if you could review the figures at your earliest convenience.
Aula 07 -- Pre-class

Pitching with Impact -- Persuasion, Influence & the Second Conditional

Sell a growth idea so the room says yes. New words: pitch, persuade, influence, value proposition, buy-in, incentive, rationale, momentum, upside, scalable, game changer, tangible. New structure: the second conditional (if + past simple, would + base verb) for an imagined, persuasive future.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The language of a persuasive pitch. Tap Listen to hear each word.

Pitch -- a short, persuasive presentation of an idea or proposal
"I have ninety seconds to pitch the kiosk idea to the board."
Persuade -- to convince someone to agree, believe, or take action
"I need to persuade the CFO that the pilot is worth funding."
Influence -- the power to shape the decisions, opinions, or behavior of others
"The regional director has real influence over the budget."
Value proposition -- the clear reason an idea is worth choosing over the alternatives
"The value proposition is simple: faster service at half the cost."
Buy-in -- agreement and active support from the people involved in a decision
"Without the CFO's buy-in, the pilot will never start."
Incentive -- something that motivates a person to act or decide in a certain way
"A faster bonus cycle is a strong incentive for the branch teams."
Rationale -- the underlying reasons or logic that justify a decision or plan
"Let me give the rationale before I show the numbers."
Momentum -- the force that keeps progress, energy, or support building over time
"Three managers already asked for this, so the momentum is there."
Upside -- the potential benefit, gain, or advantage of a choice
"The ask is small, but the upside is enormous."
Scalable -- able to grow significantly without losing efficiency or quality
"The model is fully scalable to the other LATAM regions."
Game changer -- something that dramatically shifts a situation or the way things are done
"Self-service kiosks would be a real game changer for our branches."
Tangible -- real and clearly measurable, rather than vague or abstract
"Pair every benefit with a tangible number the board can trust."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Pitch
Persuade
Influence
Value proposition
Buy-in
Incentive
Rationale
Momentum
Upside
Scalable
Game changer
Tangible

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the pitch summary and answer the questions.

The Growth Manager walked into the pre-board meeting with a single pitch: smart self-service kiosks for the busiest branches. He opened with the value proposition instead of the background. "If we placed the kiosks in those branches, we would cut transaction time in half," he said. The rationale was clear and the numbers were tangible: "If we invested two million now, we would see a return inside eighteen months, and the model is fully scalable." He knew the cost would draw pushback, so he reframed it: "If we delayed this, we would hand the advantage to a competitor." Then he kept the momentum going by shrinking the ask. "If we ran a two-branch pilot first, we would lower the risk before any rollout." The CFO smiled. The upside was obvious, the ask was small, and the idea sounded less like a gamble and more like a game changer.

1. Why does the manager say "If we placed the kiosks, we would cut transaction time" rather than "we will cut"?
A It is a second conditional: an imagined result that depends on a hypothetical action.
B It is a third conditional, describing a past that did not happen.
C It is a firm promise that the kiosks are already installed.
2. What does "If we delayed this, we would hand the advantage to a competitor" do in the pitch?
A It apologizes for the cost.
B It reframes the objection by showing the cost of waiting.
C It reports what the board already decided.
3. In a second conditional, what form follows "if"?
A "would" plus the base verb.
B The past simple (placed, invested, ran).
C "had" plus the past participle.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Second Conditional

GRAMMAR

Use it to sell an imagined present or future: a result that could still happen.

FormPatternExample
AffirmativeIf + past simple, would + base"If we invested, we would grow."
NegativeIf + did not + base, would not + base"If we did not act, we would not lead."
QuestionWhat would + subject + base, if...?"What would it cost, if we started now?"
Formal "were"If I / he / she + were"If I were the sponsor, I would fund it."
Watch the traps: the if-clause uses past simple, never "would"; the result uses "would + base", not "will". Second conditional = imagined now or future (it could happen); third conditional = unreal past (it never happened). PT: o second conditional vende um futuro hipotetico -- "se fizessemos X, conseguiriamos Y" (if + passado simples, would + verbo base); nao confunda com o third conditional, que fala de um passado que NAO aconteceu.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"If we the kiosks in the busiest branches, we would cut wait times in half."
"If the board approved the budget, we our regional reach."
"If I the sponsor, I would fund the pilot today."
"If we this, we would hand the advantage to a competitor."
"If we ran a two-branch pilot, we the risk before any rollout."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Put the steps of a persuasive pitch in the correct sequence.

?Back the hook with a tangible number using a second conditional.
?Close with a small, concrete ask, such as a two-branch pilot.
?Open with the value proposition instead of the background.
?Reframe the main objection by showing the cost of waiting.
?Deliver the hook: one vivid second-conditional benefit.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

The value proposition is simple: faster service at half the cost.
If we placed the kiosks, we would cut transaction time in half.
The bottom line is a return inside eighteen months, and it is scalable.
If we delayed this, we would hand the advantage to a competitor.
The ask is small: a two-branch pilot, scalable on success.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each pitching situation.

You want to sell an imagined benefit that depends on a hypothetical action. Which is correct?
A "If we would automate the work, we would save hours."
B "If we automated the work, we would save hundreds of hours."
C "If we automated the work, we will save hundreds of hours."
The CFO says the project is too expensive. How do you reframe the cost persuasively?
A "If we delayed it, we would hand the advantage to a competitor."
B "If we would delay it, a competitor will win."
C "If we had delayed it, we would have lost the market."
You want to open your pitch the way a CFO would respect. What do you lead with?
A A long history of the project's background.
B The value proposition and a second-conditional hook backed by a number.
C An apology for taking up the board's time.
You want to keep the ask low-risk so the board says yes. What do you propose?
A A full, company-wide rollout starting next week.
B A small two-branch pilot, scalable if it succeeds.
C No specific ask, just general interest.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Record a ninety-second pitch for a real growth idea at Brinks. Open with the value proposition and a second-conditional hook ("If we did X, we would get Y"), back it with one tangible number, reframe one likely objection, and close with a small, concrete ask. Use the second conditional at least three times and at least four words from the kit.

Survival Card -- Lesson 7

1The value proposition is simple: faster service at half the cost.
2If we placed the kiosks, we would cut transaction time in half.
3The bottom line is a return inside eighteen months, and it is scalable.
4If we delayed this, we would hand the advantage to a competitor.
5The ask is small: a two-branch pilot, scalable on success.
Aula 08 -- Pre-class

Negotiating the Deal -- Holding Your Ground & Trading Concessions

Close a deal without giving anything away for free. New words: concession, trade-off, counteroffer, deadlock, common ground, bargaining chip, non-negotiable, sticking point, meet halfway, hold firm, in good faith, give and take. New structure: the first conditional for real trades (if + present simple, will/can + base) plus the connectors unless, as long as and provided that -- contrasted with the softer second conditional from Lesson 7.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The language of trading concessions. Tap Listen to hear each word.

Concession -- something you give up or agree to in order to reach an agreement
"I can offer one concession on price, but only in exchange for volume."
Trade-off -- a balance in which you accept a disadvantage to gain an advantage
"A longer contract is the trade-off for a lower unit price."
Counteroffer -- a new offer you make in response to one you did not accept
"Their price was too high, so I came back with a counteroffer."
Deadlock -- a situation where neither side will move and the talks stop
"We hit a deadlock on price, so I changed a different variable."
Common ground -- the interests or points that both sides already agree on
"Let's start from the common ground: we both want a long-term partnership."
Bargaining chip -- something of value you can offer or hold back to gain an advantage
"Our annual volume is the strongest bargaining chip we have."
Non-negotiable -- a condition that cannot be changed, traded, or given up
"The service guarantee is non-negotiable for us."
Sticking point -- the single issue that is blocking the agreement
"Lead time is the sticking point; everything else is agreed."
Meet halfway -- to make equal compromises so that both sides gain something
"We were close, so we agreed to meet halfway on the rate."
Hold firm -- to refuse to change your position, even under pressure
"On the guarantee, I had to hold firm and not give way."
In good faith -- with honest intentions to reach a fair agreement
"Both sides came to the table in good faith."
Give and take -- mutual compromise, where each side offers something to the other
"A good deal needs give and take from both sides."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Concession
Trade-off
Counteroffer
Deadlock
Common ground
Bargaining chip
Non-negotiable
Sticking point
Meet halfway
Hold firm
In good faith
Give and take

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the negotiation summary and answer the questions.

The Growth Manager sat down with the supplier in good faith and opened from the common ground: both sides wanted a long-term partnership. He knew the talk would be all give and take, so he led with a trade-off. "If you bring the lead time down to four weeks, we will commit to the full volume," he said. That volume was his strongest bargaining chip. The supplier pushed back, and for a moment they reached a deadlock on price. So he set a clear condition: "We can't sign unless the rate drops by five percent." He offered a longer contract as a concession, but only with a condition attached: "We'll sign for two years, as long as the service guarantee stays exactly as written" -- because the guarantee was non-negotiable, and on that he had to hold firm. The only real sticking point left was three percent on the rate. So he made his final counteroffer: they would meet halfway, and the deal was done.

1. Why does the manager say "If you bring the lead time down, we will commit" rather than "If you will bring it down"?
A It is a first conditional: the if-clause uses the present simple, never "will".
B Both clauses should use "will" for emphasis.
C It is a third conditional about the past.
2. What does "We can't sign unless the rate drops" mean?
A They will sign even if the rate stays the same.
B They will only sign if the rate drops ("unless" = "if not").
C They have already signed the contract.
3. Which form correctly follows "as long as" in a real trade?
A "will" plus the base verb.
B The present simple (stays, holds, drops).
C "had" plus the past participle.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Conditionals for Trading

GRAMMAR

Use the first conditional to put a real trade on the table: a give for a get.

FormPatternExample
AffirmativeIf + present simple, will/can + base"If you cut the price, we will sign."
unless (= if not)will not + base, unless + present"We won't commit unless the lead time drops."
as long as / provided that+ present simple (one condition)"We'll renew, as long as you hold the rate."
Softer (second cond.)If + past simple, would + base"If you dropped it, we would consider it."
Watch the traps: the if-clause uses the present simple, never "will"; "unless" already means "if not", so never add a second negative. First conditional = a real offer on the table now; the second conditional (Lesson 7) is softer and more tentative, to float an idea politely. PT: o first conditional poe uma troca real na mesa -- "se voce fizer X, nos fazemos Y" (if + presente, will/can + verbo base); use o second conditional quando quiser soar mais cauteloso e diplomatico.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word or form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"If you to the annual volume, we will lock in this price."
"We can't sign the lead time comes down to four weeks."
"We'll renew for two years, you hold the current rate."
"We'll increase the order, delivery stays under five days."
"If you reduced the price a little, we revisit the volume."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Put the steps of a strong negotiation in the correct sequence.

?Name your one non-negotiable calmly and hold firm on it.
?Close by meeting halfway on the final sticking point.
?Open from the common ground you already share.
?If you hit a deadlock, change a variable instead of pushing the same point.
?Trade a concession with a first conditional: a give for a get.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Let's start from the common ground: we both want a long-term partnership.
If you bring the lead time down, we will commit to the full volume.
We can't sign unless the price drops by five percent.
The service guarantee is non-negotiable, and that is where I have to hold firm.
Let's meet halfway: if you give us three percent, we will sign today.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each negotiation situation.

You want to put a real trade on the table: a shorter lead time for the full volume. Which is correct?
A "If you will cut the lead time, we will commit to the volume."
B "If you cut the lead time, we will commit to the full volume."
C "If you cut the lead time, we commit to the full volume."
You will only sign if the price drops. How do you say it cleanly with "unless"?
A "We can't sign unless the price drops by five percent."
B "We can't sign unless the price doesn't drop by five percent."
C "Unless the price will drop, we can't sign."
The other side keeps pushing on a point where you cannot move. What is the best move?
A Give in quietly to keep the peace.
B Name it as non-negotiable, calmly, and hold firm.
C Raise your voice and threaten to walk out.
You reach a deadlock on price and neither side will move. What do you do?
A Repeat your price louder until they agree.
B Change a variable, like a longer term or bigger volume, so both sides can move.
C End the meeting with no agreement and no plan.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Record a two-minute negotiation for a real supplier deal at Brinks. Open with the common ground, trade at least two concessions using the first conditional ("If you do X, we will do Y") and a connector (unless / as long as / provided that), hold one non-negotiable calmly, and close by meeting halfway. Use a real trade at least three times and at least four words from the kit.

Survival Card -- Lesson 8

1Let's start from the common ground: we both want a long-term partnership.
2If you bring the lead time down, we will commit to the full volume.
3We can't sign unless the price drops by five percent.
4The service guarantee is non-negotiable, and that is where I have to hold firm.
5Let's meet halfway: if you give us three percent, we will sign today.
Aula 09 -- Pre-class

Cross-Cultural Communication -- Reading the Room Across Business Cultures

Interpret signals across cultures instead of assuming, and adapt your style so respect travels with you. New words: etiquette, norm, directness, hierarchy, save face, small talk, read the room, rapport, punctuality, come across as, misread, adapt. New structure: modals of deduction in the present (might / may / could = possible, must = almost sure yes, can't = almost sure no) to read behavior -- always with the base verb after the modal.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The language of reading the room. Tap Listen to hear each word.

Etiquette -- the customary rules of polite behavior in a particular setting
"Business etiquette on a first call often starts with names and titles."
Norm -- a standard or expected way of behaving that a group shares
"In some teams, the norm is to reach consensus before anyone commits."
Directness -- how openly and explicitly people say what they mean
"Some colleagues value directness; others find a softer approach more polite."
Hierarchy -- the ranking of people by seniority and authority in an organization
"In a steep hierarchy, the most senior person usually speaks last."
Save face -- to protect someone's dignity and avoid public embarrassment
"He gave them a way to save face instead of correcting them in front of the room."
Small talk -- light, informal conversation that builds rapport before business
"A few minutes of small talk about the weekend warmed up the call."
Read the room -- to sense the mood and unspoken signals of a group
"She read the room, saw the silence, and slowed right down."
Rapport -- a warm, trusting relationship that makes communication easy
"Building rapport early made the whole negotiation far smoother."
Punctuality -- the habit of being on time and strict about schedules
"Punctuality signals respect in many of our partner offices."
Come across as -- to give a particular impression to other people
"Being too direct can come across as rude in some cultures."
Misread -- to interpret a signal or situation incorrectly
"I misread his silence as disagreement, but he was just thinking."
Adapt -- to adjust your behavior to fit a new situation or culture
"Good communicators adapt their style to the people in the room."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Etiquette
Norm
Directness
Hierarchy
Save face
Small talk
Read the room
Rapport
Punctuality
Come across as
Misread
Adapt

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the call summary and answer the questions.

The Growth Manager joined a call with three offices, and he knew the etiquette would not be the same on every screen. The Frankfurt team valued directness, so a blunt "this doesn't work" was simply the norm, not an attack. "They must value clear, fast answers," he thought, so he got to the point. The Tokyo office was quieter; when they paused, he reminded himself, "That silence might mean they are still building agreement — it can't mean a flat no." He did not want to misread the room, so he gave people room to save face and never corrected anyone publicly. With the Buenos Aires team he opened with a little small talk to build rapport, because there the relationship comes first. He watched the hierarchy too: the most senior voice often spoke last, and he respected their punctuality by starting exactly on time. Above all, he tried to read the room, adapt his style, and make sure nothing he said would come across as rude.

1. Why does the manager say "They must value clear answers" rather than "They value clear answers"?
A "Must" shows it is a confident deduction from the signals, not a stated fact.
B "Must" here means they are obliged to value clear answers.
C "Must" is the only way to talk about the future.
2. What does "That silence might mean they are still building agreement" tell us?
A He is certain the silence means agreement.
B He sees it as one possible reading, not a certainty ("might" = possible).
C The silence definitely means a flat no.
3. Which form correctly follows a modal of deduction like "must" or "can't"?
A "to" plus the base verb (must to be).
B The base verb with no "to" and no "-s" (must be, can't mean).
C The past participle (must been).

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Modals of Deduction

GRAMMAR

Use modals of deduction to read a signal and say how sure you are.

CertaintyPatternExample
Possiblemight / may / could + base"They might be waiting for consensus."
Almost sure (yes)must + base"Punctuality must be important to them."
Almost sure (no)can't + base"That can't mean he disagrees."
Possibly notmay not / might not + base"They may not have the final say."
Watch the traps: after a modal, use the base verb with no "to" and no "-s" (must be, not must to be / must is). For a confident negative reading use "can't", never "mustn't" -- "mustn't" means it is forbidden, not impossible. PT: modais de deducao -- usamos might/may/could (e possivel), must (tenho quase certeza que sim) e can't (tenho quase certeza que nao) para interpretar sinais; sempre + verbo no infinitivo sem "to".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct modal or form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"They haven't answered yet — they still discussing it internally."
"She keeps checking the clock — punctuality matter a lot to them."
"A long silence isn't always bad — it mean they are still thinking."
"She runs the whole account, so she be a junior assistant."
"After a modal, always use the base verb: 'He must the decision-maker.'"

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Put the steps of reading a cross-cultural room in the correct sequence.

?Check your reading instead of guessing in silence.
?Protect the relationship: build rapport and let people save face.
?Notice the cultural signals in the room: silence, directness, timing.
?Adapt your style and your directness to each side.
?Offer a possible reading with might / may / could before you assume.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

They haven't replied yet; they might be waiting for consensus.
Punctuality must be very important to them.
That can't mean he disagrees; silence often means he is still thinking.
Too much directness can come across as blunt, so I add a little warmth.
Let me build some rapport first with a little small talk before business.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each cross-cultural situation.

The other team goes silent right after your proposal. What is the best read?
A "They must hate it" — assume the worst and move on.
B "They might be building consensus — I'll check before I assume."
C "They can't be listening, so I'll repeat it louder."
A partner says bluntly, "This doesn't work." How do you read it?
A It must be a personal attack on me.
B In a direct culture, that can be the norm, not an insult.
C They mustn't respect me at all.
Which sentence is grammatically correct for a confident reading?
A "He must to be the most senior person here."
B "He must be the most senior person here."
C "He must is the most senior person here."
A senior colleague is clearly wrong in front of their own team. What is the best move?
A Correct them immediately and publicly.
B Give them a way to save face and raise it privately later.
C Say nothing ever and let the mistake stand.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Record a two-minute cross-cultural briefing for a real three-office call at Brinks.
