Read at least three cultural signals using modals of deduction ("they might be...", "it must be...", "that can't mean..."), say how you will adapt your style for each side, and how you will build rapport and avoid misreading. Use at least four words from the kit.

Survival Card -- Lesson 9

1They haven't replied yet -- they might be waiting for consensus.
2Punctuality must be very important to them.
3That can't mean he disagrees; silence often means he is still thinking.
4Too much directness can come across as blunt, so I add a little warmth.
5Let me build some rapport first with a little small talk before business.
Aula 10 -- Pre-class

Difficult Conversations -- Handling Tension with Care

Open a hard talk without triggering defensiveness, stay candid yet constructive, and protect the relationship. New words: candid, acknowledge, empathy, defensive, constructive, morale, own up to, defuse, assertive, clear the air, address, fallout. New structure: tentative / diplomatic language (I was hoping we could..., I was wondering if..., it would help if...) plus I-statements to soften a difficult message.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The language of handling tension with care. Tap Listen to hear each word.

Candid -- honest and direct, even when the truth is uncomfortable
"I'll be candid: last quarter fell short of what we promised."
Acknowledge -- to accept and recognize a point, an effort, or a feeling
"She acknowledged his frustration before she explained the decision."
Empathy -- the ability to understand and share another person's feelings
"He led with empathy, so the conversation never turned into a fight."
Defensive -- quick to protect yourself when you feel criticized or blamed
"When feedback sounds like an attack, people get defensive and stop listening."
Constructive -- intended to help someone improve, not only to point out a flaw
"Good feedback is constructive: it points to a fix, not just a fault."
Morale -- the overall confidence, energy, and spirit of a person or team
"Handled badly, one hard talk can damage the whole team's morale."
Own up to -- to admit a mistake honestly instead of hiding or blaming
"He owned up to the missed deadline instead of blaming the tools."
Defuse -- to reduce the tension in a difficult or heated situation
"A calm question can defuse a tense moment before it grows."
Assertive -- confident and clear about your point without being aggressive
"Being assertive means stating your point firmly but respectfully."
Clear the air -- to resolve tension by talking about it openly and honestly
"They finally cleared the air and moved forward as a team."
Address -- to deal with a problem or issue directly
"It's better to address a small issue early than to let it grow."
Fallout -- the negative results that follow a conflict or a mistake
"Avoiding the talk only made the fallout worse later."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Candid
Acknowledge
Empathy
Defensive
Constructive
Morale
Own up to
Defuse
Assertive
Clear the air
Address
Fallout

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the prep note and answer the questions.

The Growth Manager had to address a drop in one team member's results, and he was dreading it. He knew that "You missed your targets" would make the man defensive and shut the talk down, so he chose a softer opening. "I was hoping we could look at the last quarter together," he said, and then, "Before I say anything, how do you see it?" Leading with a question and a little empathy helped defuse the tension. The manager stayed candid about the gap — pretending it was fine would help no one — but he kept it constructive, talking about fixes rather than blame. He was careful to acknowledge the effort first. Because the conversation felt safe, the man owned up to two slips instead of defending himself. They agreed on one next step, which protected his morale and helped them clear the air. An honest, assertive approach meant they avoided the fallout a harsher talk would have caused.

1. Why does the manager say "I was hoping we could look at the last quarter" instead of "You missed your targets"?
A A tentative form softens the opening so the other person doesn't get defensive.
B "I was hoping" means the conversation already happened in the past.
C "I was hoping" is the only correct way to start any sentence.
2. What does staying "candid but constructive" mean in the note?
A Avoiding the problem to keep things pleasant.
B Being honest about the gap while focusing on fixes, not blame.
C Telling the person exactly what they did wrong, as harshly as possible.
3. Which is the diplomatic way to raise a problem with someone?
A "You always let the team down."
B "I noticed a couple of slips — I was wondering if we could look at them."
C "We need to talk. Right now."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Tentative / Diplomatic Language

GRAMMAR

Use tentative forms and I-statements to soften a difficult message.

GoalPatternExample
Soften a requestI was hoping (that) we could + base"I was hoping we could review this together."
Ask tentativelyI was wondering if you could + base"I was wondering if you could walk me through it."
Suggest gentlyIt would help if + past / Perhaps we could + base"It would help if we set one clear owner."
Own the messageI-statement: I noticed / I felt"I noticed the tone got tense — I'd like to reset."
Watch the traps: after "I was hoping we" / "I was wondering if you", use "could" (not "can"); "it would help" takes the base verb (would help, not would helped). Swap "you always / you never" for an I-statement. PT: linguagem diplomatica -- formas no passado e com "would" criam distancia e soam mais educadas ("I was hoping", "it would help"), mesmo querendo dizer algo no presente; use frases com "I" (I noticed, I felt) em vez de acusar com "you always".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct softener or form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Instead of blaming, I said: 'I we could look at the last quarter together.'"
"I was if you'd have a moment to talk today."
"It help if we set one clear owner."
"Instead of 'You're wrong', I use an I-statement: 'I it a little differently.'"
"Perhaps we try another approach together."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Put the steps of handling a difficult conversation in the correct sequence.

?Be candid about the problem, but keep it constructive.
?Agree on one next step together to clear the air.
?Open with care: a tentative form, not an accusation.
?Help them own up without losing morale; protect the relationship.
?Lead with a question and empathy before you say anything hard.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I was hoping we could look at the last quarter together.
Before I say anything, how do you see it?
I want to be candid, and I want this to stay constructive.
A little empathy can defuse the tension before it grows.
Let's clear the air and agree on one next step together.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each difficult situation.

A team member's results have dropped. How do you open the conversation?
A "Your numbers are down. Explain yourself."
B "I was hoping we could look at the last quarter together — how do you see it?"
C "You always underdeliver lately."
The other person starts getting defensive mid-conversation. What helps most?
A Raise your voice to make the point land.
B Acknowledge their feeling, show empathy, and defuse the tension.
C List everything they have ever done wrong.
Which sentence is correct and diplomatic?
A "I was hoping we can review this together."
B "I was hoping we could review this together."
C "I was hoping we reviewing this together."
You need to stay candid but protect the relationship. What is the best move?
A Soften so much that you never mention the problem.
B Name the issue honestly, keep it constructive, and close by clearing the air.
C Be as blunt as possible so there's no doubt.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Record a two-minute difficult conversation you really need to have at Brinks. Open with a tentative form ("I was hoping...", "I was wondering if..."), lead with a question and empathy, be candid but constructive about the issue, and close by agreeing one next step to clear the air. Use at least four words from the toolkit.

Survival Card -- Lesson 10

1I was hoping we could look at the last quarter together.
2Before I say anything, how do you see it?
3I want to be candid, and I want this to stay constructive.
4I noticed something I'd like to talk through, not blame you for.
5Let's clear the air and agree on one next step together.
Aula 11 -- Pre-class

Presenting to the Board -- Slides & Stories

Tell the growth story to the board: set the backdrop, build to a turning point, switch to the dramatic present for the key moment, signpost every step, and land one clear takeaway. New words: signpost, takeaway, walk through, compelling, backdrop, storyline, turning point, drill down, headline, land, flag, frame. New structure: narrative tenses (past perfect for the backstory, past continuous for the scene, past simple for the events) plus the dramatic / historic present to make one moment vivid.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The language of presenting a story to a board. Tap Listen to hear each word.

Signpost -- to tell your audience clearly where the talk is going next
"Let me signpost the plan: headline first, then the detail."
Takeaway -- the single key point you want the audience to remember
"If there's one takeaway today, it's that the model scales."
Walk through -- to guide someone step by step through something
"Let me walk you through how we got to that number."
Compelling -- so convincing or interesting that people pay full attention
"A compelling story makes a board lean in, not check their phones."
Backdrop -- the background situation against which events happen
"Against a backdrop of a sliding market, our growth stood out."
Storyline -- the connected thread that holds a presentation together
"Each slide moved the storyline forward instead of repeating it."
Turning point -- the moment when a situation clearly changed direction
"The new route was the turning point of the whole year."
Drill down -- to move from the overview into specific detail
"Let me drill down into the two numbers behind that jump."
Headline -- to lead with your single most important number or point
"Headline the result first, then explain how you got there."
Land -- to make a message connect and stay with the audience
"Say the takeaway slowly so it really lands."
Flag -- to draw attention to something important, often in advance
"Let me flag one risk before we move on to the numbers."
Frame -- to present information from a particular angle so it makes sense
"He framed the cost as an investment, not a loss."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Signpost
Takeaway
Walk through
Compelling
Backdrop
Storyline
Turning point
Drill down
Headline
Land
Flag
Frame

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the prep note and answer the questions.

The Growth Manager had seven minutes with the board, so he decided to headline the result and then walk them through the story. First he set the backdrop: the market had been sliding for two quarters while the team was quietly rebuilding the pipeline. That context made the turning point hit harder. "In March we launched the new route," he said, and then, to make the moment vivid, he switched to the present: "So we go live, and within a week the orders double." He signposted every move so nobody got lost, paused to drill down into two numbers, and kept the storyline tight. He even flagged one risk early and framed it as manageable. The talk was compelling because it built to a single takeaway he wanted to land: the model scales to three more regions.

1. Why does the speaker say "the market had been sliding" before "we launched the new route"?
A The past perfect shows the backstory that happened BEFORE the main events.
B "Had been sliding" means it is still sliding now.
C The past perfect is only used in questions.
2. Why does he switch to "we go live, and the orders double" in the present?
A Because it is happening right now, during the presentation.
B The dramatic present makes one past moment feel vivid and immediate.
C He made a grammar mistake under pressure.
3. Which sentence correctly uses a finished past time?
A "We have launched the route last March."
B "We launched the route last March."
C "We are launching the route last March."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Narrative Tenses & the Dramatic Present

GRAMMAR

Use a different tense for each layer of the story.

Job in the storyTenseExample
The backstory (before)Past perfect: had + past participle"By March we had rebuilt the pipeline."
The scene (in progress)Past continuous: was/were + -ing"The market was sliding while we invested."
The key eventsPast simple"Then we launched the new route in March."
The vivid momentDramatic (historic) present"So we go live, and the orders double."
Watch the traps: with a finished past time (last March, in Q1) use the past simple, NOT the present perfect ("we launched", not "we have launched last March"); use "we/they were" + -ing (not "was"); use the dramatic present only for the turning point, and keep it consistent within that moment. PT: tempos narrativos -- past perfect ("had + participio") para o que veio ANTES, past continuous ("was/were + -ing") para o pano de fundo em andamento, past simple para os eventos; o presente dramatico (historic present) deixa UM momento vivo, como se acontecesse agora -- use so no ponto de virada.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct narrative form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"By the time we presented, the market already shifted."
"While we rebuilding the pipeline, revenue kept dropping."
"In March we the new route."
"So I walk in, and the whole room quiet."
"We the route last quarter."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Put the parts of a board presentation in the order a strong presenter would use.

?Build to the turning point: the key event in the past simple.
?Close on one clear takeaway and let it land.
?Headline the result and signpost where the talk is going.
?Make the moment vivid with the dramatic present, then drill down.
?Set the backdrop with the past perfect and past continuous.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Let me headline the result, then walk you through how we got there.
To put this in context, here is the backdrop.
The market had been sliding while we were rebuilding the pipeline.
So we go live, and within a week the orders double.
If there's one takeaway today, it's this.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each presenting situation.

You have seven minutes with the board. How do you open?
A Save the key result for the very end to build suspense.
B Headline the result, then signpost how you'll walk them through it.
C Start with twenty slides of raw data, no structure.
You want to tell what happened BEFORE the main events. Which is correct?
A "Before we launched, we have rebuilt the pipeline."
B "Before we launched, we had rebuilt the pipeline."
C "Before we launched, we are rebuilding the pipeline."
Which sentence correctly uses the past simple with a finished time?
A "We have signed the contract last quarter."
B "We signed the contract last quarter."
C "We were signing the contract last quarter, once."
You want one moment to feel vivid and immediate. What's the best move?
A Read every number on the slide out loud.
B Switch to the dramatic present for the turning point: "so we go live, and the orders double."
C Use the present tense for the entire presentation.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Record a two-minute board presentation of a real Brink's growth story. Headline the result, set the backdrop with narrative tenses (past perfect + past continuous), build to a turning point in the past simple, switch to the dramatic present for one vivid moment, signpost throughout, and close on one clear takeaway. Use at least four words from the toolkit.

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1Let me headline the result, then walk you through how we got there.
2To put this in context, here is the backdrop.
3I'd like to draw your attention to this turning point.
4Let me drill down into the numbers behind that.
5If there's one takeaway today, it's this.
Aula 12 -- Pre-class

The Stakeholder Meeting -- Review & Mock Meeting

Checkpoint for Blocks 7–11. Run a full mock stakeholder meeting that pulls every skill together: pitch with the second conditional, negotiate with the first conditional, read the room with modals of deduction, handle tension with tentative language, and tell the story with narrative tenses and signposting. Review words: value proposition, buy-in, trade-off, common ground, read the room, rapport, defuse, acknowledge, signpost, takeaway, push back, milestone. Grammar focus: the key contrast between the second conditional (imagined pitch) and the first conditional (real offer), plus modals of deduction and tentative language used at the right moment.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

A recap of the go-to words from Blocks 7–11 — the language that carries a stakeholder meeting. Tap Listen to hear each word.

Value proposition -- the clear reason your idea or offer is worth choosing
"Our value proposition is simple: one route, three new regions."
Buy-in -- agreement and support from the people who matter
"I need the CFO's buy-in before I take this to the board."
Trade-off -- giving up one thing in order to gain something else
"The trade-off is a slower launch for a safer one."
Common ground -- an area where two sides already agree and can build
"We found common ground on the timeline, then negotiated the budget."
Read the room -- to sense the mood and unspoken signals of the people present
"I read the room and softened the ask when I saw them tense up."
Rapport -- a friendly connection that makes people trust and listen to you
"A minute of rapport at the start makes a hard ask much easier."
Defuse -- to calm down a tense situation before it escalates
"A little empathy defused the tension before it became a fight."
Acknowledge -- to openly recognize a point, a feeling, or a valid concern
"I acknowledged her concern first, then explained the plan."
Signpost -- to tell your audience clearly where the talk is going next
"Let me signpost the plan: headline first, then the detail."
Takeaway -- the single key point you want the audience to remember
"If there's one takeaway today, it's that the model scales."
Push back -- to politely resist or question a proposal
"The CFO will push back on the budget, so I came prepared."
Milestone -- a checkpoint that marks progress toward a bigger goal
"We'll set ninety-day milestones so the board can track the pilot."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Value proposition
Buy-in
Trade-off
Common ground
Read the room
Rapport
Defuse
Acknowledge
Signpost
Takeaway
Push back
Milestone

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the meeting prep and answer the questions.

The Growth Manager opened the stakeholder meeting with the story: the market had been sliding while the team rebuilt the pipeline, then in March they launched the new route. To pitch the next step, he used an imagined case: "If we funded two more regions, we would triple the reach." He signposted his value proposition and aimed for early buy-in. When the CFO began to push back on the budget, he moved to a real offer: "If you approve the pilot, we will share milestones in ninety days." She went quiet, so he read the signal: "You might be weighing the risk." He stayed tentative to defuse the tension: "I was hoping we could explore the timing together." He acknowledged her concern, found common ground, and closed on one takeaway: start with one region.

1. Why does he say "If we funded… we would triple" for the pitch but "If you approve… we will share" for the offer?
A The second conditional pitches an imagined idea; the first conditional makes a real offer on the table.
B They mean exactly the same thing; he just varies the words.
C "Would" is always more polite than "will."
2. Why does he choose "you might be weighing the risk" instead of "you must be rejecting it"?
A "Might" and "must" mean the same level of certainty.
B The signal is weak, so "might" deduces possibility without overstating.
C "Must" is grammatically wrong after a comma.
3. Which line is the most tentative way to handle the tension?
A "You need to decide on the budget now."
B "I was hoping we could explore the timing together."
C "If you don't approve this, the plan fails."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Five Meeting Moves

GRAMMAR

Each move in a stakeholder meeting has its own structure.

MoveStructureExample
Pitch (imagined)2nd conditional: if + past, would + base"If we funded it, we would triple the reach."
Negotiate (real offer)1st conditional: if + present, will + base"If you approve it, we will share milestones."
Read the roomModals: must / might / can't + base"She might be weighing the risk."
Handle tensionTentative: I was hoping / I was wondering / could"I was hoping we could explore one option."
Tell the storyNarrative tenses + dramatic present"It had been sliding, then we launched, and so we go live."
Watch the trap: keep the two conditionals clean. A real offer on the table takes the first conditional ("if you approve, we WILL"); an imagined idea takes the second ("if we funded, we WOULD"). Use "might" for a weak signal and "must" only when the evidence is strong. PT: cada movimento da reuniao tem sua estrutura -- second conditional ("if + passado, would") para o que e imaginado (pitch), first conditional ("if + presente, will") para uma oferta REAL na mesa; modais (must/might/can't) para deduzir sinais; formas tentativas ("I was hoping... could") para suavizar; e tempos narrativos para contar a historia. Nao troque will por would entre a oferta e o pitch.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form for the move. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

Pitch: "If we opened a second hub, we cut delivery times."
Deal: "If you approve the pilot, we report milestones monthly."
Read: "He keeps checking the time, so he be under pressure."
Tension: "I was hoping we find a middle path."
Story: "By March we rebuilt the pipeline, then we launched."

Stage 2: Put the Meeting in Order

Order

Put the moves of a stakeholder meeting in the order a strong chair would use.

?Negotiate a real offer with the first conditional and milestones.
?Acknowledge concerns, find common ground, and close on one takeaway.
?Tell the quarter as a story and headline the result.
?Read the room with a modal and soften any tension with tentative language.
?Pitch the growth idea with the second conditional.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

If we funded two regions, we would triple the reach.
If you approve the pilot, we will share milestones in ninety days.
She has gone quiet, so she might be weighing the risk.
I was hoping we could explore one option together.
Let me headline the result, then walk you through the story.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each meeting situation.

You want to pitch an imagined growth idea. Which is correct?
A "If we added a region, we will double the reach."
B "If we added a region, we would double the reach."
C "If we would add a region, we double the reach."
You are making a real offer on the table. Which is correct?
A "If you approved the pilot, we would start Monday."
B "If you approve the pilot, we will start Monday."
C "If you will approve the pilot, we start Monday."
A stakeholder goes silent after your ask. You see only a weak signal. What do you say?
A "You must hate the idea."
B "You might be weighing the risk — shall we talk it through?"
C "You can't be serious about this."
The room is getting tense. How do you soften your ask?
A "You have to decide right now."
B "I was hoping we could look at the options together."
C "There's no other option, so let's move on."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Record a two-minute mock stakeholder meeting for a real Brink's growth plan. Use all five moves: tell the story with narrative tenses, pitch with the second conditional, negotiate a pilot with the first conditional, read the room with a modal, and soften one tense moment with tentative language. Close on one clear takeaway. Use at least four review words.

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1Let me headline the result, then walk you through the story.
2If we funded two regions, we would triple the reach.
3If you approve the pilot, we will share milestones in ninety days.
4She's gone quiet, so she might be weighing the risk.
5I was hoping we could explore one option together.
Aula 13 -- Pre-class

Handling Q&A -- Tough Questions Under Pressure

Field tough questions under pressure without losing your footing. Buy a moment instead of answering off the cuff, clarify a vague or loaded question with an indirect question, hedge anything you are not certain about with a qualifier, and defer cleanly when you cannot commit. New words: loaded question, probe, hedge, ballpark, defer, circle back, fielding, pin down, off the cuff, qualifier, presumptuous, caveat. Grammar focus: indirect questions (normal word order, no do/does), hedging language, and time-buying phrases.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The language that keeps a Q&A calm and credible. Tap Listen to hear each word.

Loaded question -- a question built on a hidden assumption or trap
"Why is the rollout already behind? was a loaded question."
Probe -- to ask follow-up questions that dig deeper into a vague or loaded point
"I probed the question to find out what she was really asking."
Hedge -- to answer carefully, without committing to what you are unsure of
"I hedged the number: as far as I can tell, it's around fifteen percent."
Ballpark -- a rough, approximate figure rather than an exact one
"I gave a ballpark and promised the precise number later."
Defer -- to put off an answer until later, openly and on purpose
"I deferred the exact figure to after the session."
Circle back -- to return to a point or question later, once you have the answer
"Let me check and circle back to you after lunch."
Fielding -- taking questions as they come and answering on the spot
"Fielding questions live is harder than the talk itself."
Pin down -- to press someone for an exact, committed answer
"He tried to pin me down on a date before I had the data."
Off the cuff -- spoken without preparation, on the spur of the moment
"I answered off the cuff and overcommitted — never again."
Qualifier -- a word or phrase that limits how certain a statement is
"As far as I can tell is a qualifier that signals honest doubt."
Presumptuous -- assuming something is true when it is not, taking too much for granted
"The question was presumptuous, so I corrected the premise first."
Caveat -- a warning or condition you attach to limit a statement
"With one caveat: that figure assumes the pilot holds."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Loaded question
Probe
Hedge
Ballpark
Defer
Circle back
Fielding
Pin down
Off the cuff
Qualifier
Presumptuous
Caveat

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the Q&A debrief and answer the questions.

The Growth Manager finished his slides, but the real test was fielding the questions. A board member opened with a loaded question about risk, so instead of answering off the cuff, he bought a second: "That's a fair question. Let me think." Then he used an indirect question to clarify: "Could you tell me what you mean by exposure?" Once he understood it, he hedged the part he was unsure of with a qualifier: "As far as I can tell, the risk is contained." When she tried to pin him down on an exact figure, he gave a ballpark and deferred the precise number: "I'll confirm it and circle back." Finally, when a follow-up was presumptuous — it assumed the pilot had already failed — he added a caveat and corrected the premise before answering.

1. Why does he say "Could you tell me what you mean by exposure?" instead of "What do you mean?"
A The indirect question is more polite and gives him room to clarify a loaded question.
B The two are identical; he just prefers longer sentences.
C "What do you mean?" is grammatically wrong.
2. Why does he add a qualifier ("as far as I can tell") to his answer about the risk?
A To sound less confident on purpose, even when he is sure.
B To hedge honestly — signalling he is fairly sure but has not fully confirmed it.
C Because qualifiers are required in every business sentence.
3. What does he do when she tries to pin him down on an exact figure?
A He invents a precise number on the spot to look confident.
B He gives a ballpark and defers the precise figure, promising to circle back.
C He refuses to answer and changes the subject.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Q&A Toolkit

GRAMMAR

Each move in a tough Q&A has its own structure.

ToolStructureExample
Buy timeThat's a fair question + Let me think / make sure I understand"That's a fair question. Let me think for a second."
Indirect questionCould you tell me + normal word order (no inversion, no do/does)"Could you tell me what the cost is?"
ClarifyCould you clarify whether / what you mean by…"Could you clarify whether you mean cost or timing?"
Hedge (qualifier)As far as I can tell / To the best of my knowledge + statement"As far as I can tell, the risk is contained."
Defer cleanlyI don't have X to hand, but I'll circle back / get you the figure"I'll circle back with the precise number today."
Watch the trap: in an indirect question the verb does NOT invert and you drop do/does: "Could you tell me what the cost IS" (not "what is the cost"). A hedge is honest, not weak. And buying time is not stalling. PT: cada movimento de um Q&A dificil tem sua estrutura -- ganhe um segundo ("that's a fair question, let me think"), use indirect questions (ordem normal, sem inversao e sem do/does) para perguntar com educacao, faca hedge do que nao tem certeza com um qualifier ("as far as I can tell"), e adie de forma limpa ("I'll circle back"), sempre com um compromisso. Nunca responda off the cuff nem prometa demais.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form for the tool. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

Indirect: "Could you tell me what the deadline on your side?"
Clarify: "Could you clarify you mean cost or timing?"
Hedge: " far as I can tell, the numbers hold."
Buy time: "That's a fair question. Let me for a second."
Defer: "I don't have it to hand, but I'll back after the session."

Stage 2: Put the Q&A in Order

Order

Put the steps of handling one tough question in the order a calm speaker would use.

?Hedge anything you are not certain of with a qualifier.
?Buy a second instead of answering off the cuff.
?Defer the exact figure cleanly, with a promise to circle back.
?Clarify a vague or loaded question with an indirect question.
?If a follow-up is presumptuous, correct the premise with a caveat.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

That's a fair question. Let me think for a second.
Could you clarify what you mean by exposure?
As far as I can tell, the risk is contained.
I don't have the exact figure, but I'll circle back today.
With one caveat: that assumes the pilot has failed.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each Q&A situation.

A hard question lands and you need a moment. Which is best?
A Answer instantly so you look confident.
B "That's a fair question. Let me make sure I understand it."
C "I'd rather not answer that."
You want to ask politely what the budget is. Which is correct?
A "Could you tell me what is the budget?"
B "Could you tell me what the budget is?"
C "Could you tell me what does the budget?"
You are not fully sure of a number. How do you answer honestly?
A "It's definitely exactly twelve percent, no doubt."
B "As far as I can tell, it's around twelve percent — let me confirm."
C "I have no idea, sorry."
They try to pin you down on an exact figure you don't have. What do you say?
A Make up a precise number on the spot.
B "I'll give a ballpark now and circle back with the exact figure today."
C "You'll have to ask someone else."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Record yourself fielding three tough questions about a real Brink's project. For each one, use a different tool: buy a second, clarify with an indirect question, hedge with a qualifier, or defer with a commitment to circle back. Correct one presumptuous premise with a caveat. Use at least four of the new words.

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1That's a fair question. Let me think for a second.
2Could you clarify what you mean by exposure?
3As far as I can tell, the risk is contained.
4I don't have the exact figure, but I'll circle back today.
5With one caveat: that assumes the pilot has failed.
Aula 14 -- Pre-class

Damage Control -- Delivering Bad News & Reassuring Stakeholders

Deliver bad news the right way and steady the room. Lead in with an honest softening opener, state the setback plainly, take ownership in active voice without over-apologizing, then reassure with a concrete forward plan. New words: setback, shortfall, mitigate, contingency, reassure, accountability, transparent, root cause, on track, course-correct, downside, head off. Grammar focus: active voice for owning a miss (vs passive blame-shifting), will for firm promises, going to / be + -ing for arranged steps, and the first conditional for contingencies.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The language that delivers bad news cleanly and keeps trust. Tap Listen to hear each word.

Setback -- a problem that delays or reverses progress on a plan
"The supplier delay was a real setback for the rollout."
Shortfall -- the amount by which a result falls below the target or budget
"We had a shortfall of about ten percent against the target."
Mitigate -- to reduce the seriousness or impact of a problem
"We mitigated the loss by shifting onto two core accounts."
Contingency -- a backup plan ready in case the first plan fails
"Our contingency is to fall back on the reserve budget."
Reassure -- to remove someone's worry and restore their confidence
"I reassured the client that the core service would not be touched."
Accountability -- being answerable for a result and willing to own the outcome
"Accountability means I name the miss before anyone asks."
Transparent -- open and honest, sharing the full picture without hiding it
"Being transparent about the shortfall earned me more trust."
Root cause -- the real, underlying reason behind a problem, not the symptom
"The root cause was a late forecast, not the team's effort."
On track -- progressing as planned, likely to meet the goal on time
"After the fix, the recovery plan is back on track."
Course-correct -- to adjust the plan to get back toward the goal after a miss
"We're meeting the supplier on Thursday to course-correct."
Downside -- the worst-case loss or risk if things go wrong
"I want to know the downside is covered if Thursday fails."
Head off -- to prevent a problem before it has the chance to happen
"A summary tonight will head off any surprises at the board."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Setback
Shortfall
Mitigate
Contingency
Reassure
Accountability
Transparent
Root cause
On track
Course-correct
Downside
Head off

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the damage-control debrief and answer the questions.

The quarter came in below plan, so the Growth Manager had to call a key stakeholder before the board did. He led in honestly: "There's no easy way to say this." Then he owned it in active voice — "We missed the target" — instead of the passive blame-shift "mistakes were made." He showed accountability, named the root cause, and stayed transparent about the shortfall. To reassure her, he switched to the future: "We will close the shortfall by quarter end" (a firm promise), and "We're meeting the supplier on Thursday to course-correct" (an arranged step). To mitigate the downside, he added a contingency with the first conditional: "If the pilot slips again, we will pull the reserve." A one-page summary that night would head off any surprises and keep the recovery on track.

1. Why does he say "We missed the target" instead of "Mistakes were made"?
A Active voice owns the miss directly; the passive shifts blame and sounds evasive.
B The two mean the same; he just likes shorter sentences.
C "Mistakes were made" is grammatically wrong.
2. Why does he use "will" for closing the shortfall but "we're meeting" for Thursday?
A The two are interchangeable; he chose at random.
B "Will" is a firm promise; "we're meeting" is an arranged step already fixed (be + -ing).
C "Will" is past and "we're meeting" is future.
3. How does he cover the downside if Thursday does not work?
A He promises nothing will ever go wrong.
B He adds a first-conditional contingency: if the pilot slips, they will pull the reserve.
C He refuses to discuss the downside.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Damage-Control Toolkit

GRAMMAR

Each move in delivering bad news has its own structure.

MoveStructureExample
Lead inSoftening opener + the plain fact"There's no easy way to say this: we came in below plan."
Own it (active)We + past simple, active voice + I take responsibility"We missed the target, and I take responsibility."
Firm promisewill + base verb"We will close the shortfall by quarter end."
Arranged stepgoing to / be + -ing (a fixed plan)"We're meeting the supplier on Thursday."
ContingencyIf + present simple, … will + base"If the pilot slips, we will pull the reserve."
Watch the trap: own the miss in ACTIVE voice ("we missed it"), never passive blame-shifting ("mistakes were made"). Apologize once, not five times. In the first conditional, the if-clause stays in the present, NOT "if it will slip". PT: cada movimento de uma ma noticia tem sua estrutura -- abra com um lead-in honesto, assuma o erro na voz ativa (nunca no passivo "mistakes were made", que soa evasivo), peca desculpa UMA vez, e ai pivote para o futuro: will para uma promessa firme, going to / be + -ing para um passo ja agendado, e o first conditional (if + presente, will + base) para a contingencia. Seja transparente sobre o passado e concreto sobre o futuro.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form for the move. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

Own it: "We the target, and I take responsibility for it."
Promise: "We close the shortfall by quarter end."
Arranged: "We're the supplier on Thursday to course-correct."
Contingency: " the pilot slips again, we will pull the reserve."
Lead in: "There's no easy to say this."

Stage 2: Put the Delivery in Order

Order

Put the steps of a clean bad-news delivery in the order a steady speaker would use.

?Make a firm promise with will to fix the miss.
?Lead in with an honest softening opener.
?Name an arranged step with be + -ing to course-correct.
?Own the miss in active voice and take responsibility.
?Add a first-conditional contingency to cover the downside.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

There's no easy way to say this: we came in below plan.
We missed the target, and I take responsibility for it.
We will close the shortfall by quarter end.
We're meeting the supplier on Thursday to course-correct.
If the pilot slips again, we will pull the stopgap budget.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each damage-control situation.

You have to open with bad news. Which lead-in is best?
A Bury it on slide nine and hope nobody notices.
B "I want to be straight with you: we came in below plan."
C "Everything is totally fine, by the way."
You need to own the miss. Which is the active, accountable version?
A "Mistakes were made and the target was missed."
B "We missed the target, and I take responsibility."
C "The market moved against us, so it's not on us."
A meeting is already booked for Thursday. How do you mention it?
A "We might possibly meet someone at some point."
B "We're meeting the supplier on Thursday to course-correct."
C "We met the supplier on Thursday." (it hasn't happened yet)
You want to cover the downside if the plan slips. Which is correct?
A "If the pilot will slip, we will pull the reserve."
B "If the pilot slips, we will pull the reserve."
C "If the pilot slips, we pulling the reserve."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Record yourself delivering one real piece of bad news about a Brink's project. Lead in with an honest opener, own the miss in active voice, apologize once, then reassure: a will-promise, an arranged step in be + -ing, and a first-conditional contingency to cover the downside. Use at least four of the new words.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1There's no easy way to say this: we came in below plan.
2We missed the target, and I take responsibility for it.
3We will close the shortfall by quarter end.
4We're meeting the supplier on Thursday to course-correct.
5If the pilot slips again, we will pull the stopgap budget.
Aula 15 -- Pre-class

Building Consensus -- Aligning a Divided Room

Align a divided room without steamrolling anyone. Acknowledge both sides fairly, name the common ground everyone shares, propose a way forward with tentative language, build on others' points instead of overriding them, then confirm a real yes. New words: align, faction, middle ground, concede, reconcile, stalemate, win-win, converge, give ground, broker, unanimous, lukewarm. Grammar focus: tentative, inviting proposals — "what if we + past simple", "suppose we", "I'd suggest", "we could" — versus orders, plus acknowledging language and checking for real buy-in.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The language that aligns a divided room and reaches a real yes. Tap Listen to hear each word.

Align -- to bring people or teams into agreement on a direction
"We need ops and growth to align before the board meets."
Faction -- a small group within a larger one that pushes its own side
"Two factions formed: one for caution, one for speed."
Middle ground -- a position that both sides can partly accept
"The middle ground was to fund one region, not all four."
Concede -- to give up a point or claim in order to reach agreement
"Growth conceded the timeline; ops conceded on budget."
Reconcile -- to make two opposing positions or goals fit together
"We reconciled the margin target with the growth plan."
Stalemate -- a deadlock in which neither side will give way
"We were in a stalemate until someone named the shared goal."
Win-win -- an outcome in which both sides gain something they value
"Phasing the spend was a win-win: speed and a safe margin."
Converge -- to move from different positions toward a single one
"Once we shared the goal, the two sides began to converge."
Give ground -- to move from a fixed position and accept part of the other view
"When people feel heard, they give ground more easily."
Broker -- to arrange an agreement between two sides who disagree
"I'm trying to broker a deal between the two teams."
Unanimous -- agreed by absolutely everyone, with no one against
"It wasn't unanimous, but everyone could live with it."
Lukewarm -- only mildly supportive; not a real, committed yes
"A lukewarm nod isn't consensus — it comes back next week."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Align
Faction
Middle ground
Concede
Reconcile
Stalemate
Win-win
Converge
Give ground
Broker
Unanimous
Lukewarm

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the consensus-building debrief and answer the questions.

The leadership room was split into two factions: operations wanted to protect the margin, growth wanted to fund new regions. The Growth Manager had to broker a way out of the stalemate. First he acknowledged both sides — "there's merit in both positions" — so people felt heard and would give ground. Then he named the common ground to make the room converge: "Where we all agree is a healthy company in a year." Only then did he propose, and tentatively: "What if we funded one region first?" and "Suppose we capped the spend." Notice the past simple in "funded" — it is not real past time; it is a softener that turns an order into an invitation. Each side conceded a little, they found the middle ground, and the goals were reconciled into a win-win. Before moving on, he checked for a real yes, not a lukewarm one: "Can everyone live with that?" It wasn't unanimous enthusiasm, but it was a decision everyone owned — the two teams had genuinely aligned.

1. Why does he acknowledge both sides BEFORE proposing anything?
A When people feel heard, they relax and give ground instead of digging in.
B It is just polite; it has no effect on the outcome.
C So he can avoid stating his own opinion at all.
2. Why does he say "What if we funded" instead of "We will fund"?
A "Funded" is past, so the plan already happened.
B The past simple is a softener; it makes a proposal feel like an invitation, not an order.
C The two mean the same; he chose at random.
3. Why does he ask "Can everyone live with that?" at the end?
A To restart the whole debate from scratch.
B To confirm a real yes the room owns, not a lukewarm nod that falls apart later.
C Because he is unsure of his own proposal.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Consensus Toolkit

GRAMMAR

Each move in aligning a divided room has its own structure.

MoveStructureExample
AcknowledgeThere's merit in… / I hear both sides"There's merit in both positions."
ConvergeWhere we all agree is… / what we share is"Where we all agree is a healthy company."
Propose (soft)What if we + past simple / suppose we / I'd suggest / we could"What if we funded one region first?"
Build onBuilding on X's point… / to add to that"Building on Adriana's point, we could cap it."
ConfirmCan everyone live with…? / are we all on board?"Can everyone live with that?"
Watch the trap: in a divided room, an order ("we will fund all four") shuts people down; a tentative proposal ("what if we funded one?") invites them in. The past simple in "what if we funded" is NOT real past time — it's a softener, the same job done by "suppose we", "I'd suggest", and "we could". And never assume agreement: always confirm a real yes, because a lukewarm nod is not consensus. PT: cada movimento para alinhar uma sala dividida tem sua estrutura -- reconheca os dois lados primeiro (para as pessoas darem espaco), nomeie o terreno comum, e SO ENTAO proponha, de forma tentativa: "what if we + passado simples", "suppose we", "I'd suggest", "we could". O passado simples em "what if we funded" nao e tempo passado de verdade, e um amaciador que transforma uma ordem em convite. Por fim, confirme um sim de verdade -- um aceno morno (lukewarm) nao e consenso e volta na semana seguinte.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form for the move. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

Acknowledge: "There's in both positions."
Converge: "Where we all is a healthy company in a year."
Propose: "What if we one region and reviewed it at half-year?"
Build on: " on your point, we could cap the spend."
Confirm: "Can everyone with that?"

Stage 2: Put the Alignment in Order

Order

Put the moves of aligning a divided room in the order a skilled broker would use.

?Propose the middle ground tentatively with "what if we".
?Acknowledge both sides so people feel heard.
?Build on someone's point instead of overriding it.
?Converge on the goal everyone shares.
?Confirm a real yes everyone can live with.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I hear both sides, and there's merit in each.
Where we all agree is a healthy company in a year.
What if we funded one region and reviewed it at half-year?
Building on your point, we could cap the spend.
Can everyone genuinely live with that, not a lukewarm yes?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each consensus-building situation.

The room is split into two factions. How do you open?
A "My side is right, so let's stop wasting time."
B "I hear both sides, and there's real merit in each."
C "Let's just vote and move on."
You want to propose the middle ground without imposing it. Which is best?
A "We will fund one region. That's decided."
B "What if we funded one region and reviewed it at half-year?"
C "You must accept funding one region."
Which "what if" proposal is grammatically correct?
A "What if we will cap the spend?"
B "What if we capped the spend?"
C "What if we capping the spend?"
You sense only a lukewarm nod. What do you do before locking the decision?
A "Great, we all agree. Moving on." (nobody clearly said yes)
B "Can everyone genuinely live with this — a real yes?"
C "If no one objects loudly, it's decided."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Record yourself aligning a real divided decision at Brink's. Acknowledge both factions, name the common ground, propose the middle ground with "what if + past simple", build on at least one point, then confirm a genuine yes. Use at least four of the new words.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1I hear both sides, and there's merit in each.
2Where we all agree is a healthy company in a year.
3What if we funded one region and reviewed it at half-year?
4Building on your point, we could cap the spend.
5Can everyone genuinely live with that, not a lukewarm yes?
Aula 16 -- Pre-class

Influencing Without Authority -- Getting Buy-In Across the Org

Get buy-in from people who do not report to you. Find the win for them first, frame so they own the how instead of giving orders, enlist an ally to champion the idea before the meeting, rally everyone around a shared goal, and use reciprocity. New words: advocate, champion, ally, enlist, rally, sway, endorse, reciprocity. Grammar focus: causative structures — "get someone to do" and "have someone do" — plus purpose clauses "so that" and "in order to" that give the reason people want to act.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The language that moves people you cannot order. Tap Listen to hear each word.

Advocate -- to speak up publicly in support of an idea or person
"I need someone in finance to advocate for the rollout."
Champion -- to strongly back an idea and actively push it forward for others
"Ask the most credible person in the room to champion it."
Ally -- a person on your side who supports your cause
"Find one ally before the meeting; never go in cold."
Enlist -- to get someone to join your effort and lend their support
"I enlisted IT early so they shaped the plan, not just received it."
Rally -- to bring people together in support of a shared goal
"I rallied all three teams around the regional target."
Sway -- to influence or shift someone's opinion in your direction
"Their numbers swayed the room more than my slides did."
Endorse -- to publicly and officially give your support to an idea
"Once the director endorsed it, the others fell in line."
Reciprocity -- a two-way exchange of favors — you help them, they help you
"Reciprocity is real: back their priorities and they back yours."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Advocate
Champion
Ally
Enlist
Rally
Sway
Endorse
Reciprocity

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the influence debrief and answer the questions.

The Growth Manager needed three teams for the regional rollout, and not one of them reported to him. He could not give orders, so he had to sway people who owed him nothing. First he found the win for each side — a cleaner forecast for finance, fewer fire drills for IT. Then, instead of commanding, he got people to act: "I got finance to back the plan" and "I'll have IT review it" — notice that after get we use to + verb, but after have we use the bare verb, with no to. He framed every request with a reason: "I shaped it so that they owned the how," and "I met them early in order to enlist their support." He enlisted one ally before the meeting and asked her to champion the idea, because one advocate in the room is worth ten slides. Finally he rallied the teams around a shared target and relied on reciprocity — he backed their priorities first, so they would back his. By the end, every team endorsed the plan as if it were their own.

1. Why does he say "I got finance TO back the plan" instead of "Back the plan"?
A He has no authority over finance; "get someone to" persuades, an order would fail.
B "Get someone to" and an order mean exactly the same thing.
C Because finance reports directly to him.
2. Which form is correct after "have" in the causative?
A "I'll have IT to review it." (have + to + verb)
B "I'll have IT review it." (have + bare verb, no "to")
C "I'll have IT reviewing it." (have + -ing)
3. What does "so that" add to "I shaped it so that they owned the how"?
A Nothing; it's just filler.
B The reason/result — the "why" that makes people want to act.
C It turns the sentence into a question.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Causatives & Purpose Clauses

GRAMMAR

When you cannot order, you get people to act — and give the reason why.

StructureFormExample
Get + person + to + verbpersuade / lead someone to act"I got finance to back it."
Have + person + verbarrange for someone to do it (NO "to")"I'll have IT review the plan."
… so that + clausethe reason / result (the "why")"I framed it so that they owned it."
… in order to + verbthe purpose (formal)"I met them early in order to enlist support."
Watch the trap: after get you DO use "to" ("get them to agree"); after have you do NOT ("have them agree"). And always attach the reason with "so that + clause" or "in order to + verb" — that "why" is what turns a request into something a peer actually wants to do. PT: quando voce nao pode mandar, voce CONSEGUE que a pessoa aja, em vez de ordenar. Use "get + pessoa + TO + verbo" ("I got them to agree") e "have + pessoa + verbo" SEM "to" ("I'll have IT review it"). Cuidado: depois de GET usa-se "to"; depois de HAVE, NAO. E sempre amarre o porque com "so that" + oracao ("so that they own it") ou "in order to" + verbo ("in order to win support"). Essa razao e o que transforma um pedido em algo que um colega quer fazer.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

Persuade: "I got finance back the rollout."
Arrange: "I'll have IT the plan first."
Reason: "I framed it that they owned the how."
Purpose: "I met them early in to enlist their support."
Enlist: "Could I get you champion this in the meeting?"

Stage 2: Put the Campaign in Order

Order

Put the moves of influencing without authority in the order a skilled operator would use.

?Enlist an ally and get them to champion the idea early.
?Find the win for the other team before you ask.
?Rally everyone around the goal you all share.
?Frame so they own the how, not give orders.
?Use reciprocity: back their priorities so they back yours.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

What's in it for your team is fewer last-minute fire drills.
What would have to be true for this to work for you?
Could I get you to champion this early in the room?
I'll have IT review the plan so that we avoid surprises.
I'll support your project first, so you can count on me too.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each influence situation.

You need a peer's team to help, and they don't report to you. How do you open?
A "I need this done, so make it happen."
B "What's in it for your team is fewer last-minute fire drills."
C "My plan is approved, so you have to support it."
Which causative sentence is correct?
A "I'll have IT to review the plan."
B "I'll have IT review the plan."
C "I got finance back the rollout."
You want a credible peer to support your idea publicly in the meeting. What's the best ask?
A "You will present this for me."
B "Could I get you to champion this early in the room?"
C "Just agree with whatever I say."
How do you make influence run both ways over time?
A Only ask for favors; never give them.
B Use reciprocity — back their priorities first, so they back yours later.
C Remind them you outrank them.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Record yourself winning over a peer at Brink's who doesn't report to you. Find their win, frame it with "get them to" and "so that", enlist one ally to champion it, rally around a shared goal, and offer reciprocity. Use at least four of the new words.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1What's in it for your team is fewer last-minute fire drills.
2What would have to be true for this to work for you?
3Could I get you to champion this early in the room?
4Let's tie this to the target we all share.
5I'll support your project first, so you can count on me too.
Aula 17 -- Pre-class

Leading the Room -- Chairing & Steering a Meeting

Chair and steer a meeting from start to finish. Open with a clear purpose and a timed agenda, manage the floor by bringing quiet people in and parking digressions as sidebars, protect the clock, drive the room to a decision, and close with a recap of decisions and owners before you adjourn. New words: convene, agenda, quorum, steer, digress, sidebar, recap, adjourn. Grammar focus: chairing language — soft imperatives "shall we / why don't we + bare verb", polite control "would you mind + -ing" and "could we + verb", plus steering markers "let's move on" and "in the interest of time".
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The language a chair uses to run a room. Tap Listen to hear each word.

Convene -- to formally call people together to hold a meeting
"I'll convene the regional leads on Thursday at ten."
Agenda -- the planned list of items a meeting will work through
"Three items on the agenda, a time box on each."
Quorum -- the minimum number of people needed for a decision to count
"We have quorum, so we can decide today."
Steer -- to guide a meeting in the right direction and keep it on course
"When it drifts, I steer us back to the decision."
Digress -- to wander away from the main topic of the discussion
"He started to digress, so I gently brought us back."
Sidebar -- a side discussion set aside to deal with separately, not in the main meeting
"Let's take that offline as a sidebar after the call."
Recap -- to briefly go back over the main points and decisions at the end
"Let me recap: here's what we decided and who owns it."
Adjourn -- to formally end a meeting, usually on time
"We've decided everything; let's adjourn on time."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Convene
Agenda
Quorum
Steer
Digress
Sidebar
Recap
Adjourn

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the chairing debrief and answer the questions.

The Growth Manager convened the regional review with three items on the agenda and a time box on each. He opened with the purpose, then ran the floor like a job. When a lead began to digress into the budget, he did not bark an order; he said, "Would you mind parking that as a sidebar for after?" — notice that after would you mind we use the -ing form, not to. To bring a quiet colleague in he asked, "Why don't we hear from Carla?" and to keep moving he said, "Shall we move on to the next item?" — after why don't we and shall we, the verb is bare, with no to. Whenever the room drifted, he guarded the clock: "In the interest of time, could we come back to that later?" Finally he refused to let it end loose: "Let me recap the decisions and owners," and once it was clear, he adjourned on time. The room had quorum, so the date was decided that hour — and for once nobody left unsure of what had been agreed.

1. Why does he say "Would you mind parking that?" instead of "Stop talking about budget"?
A A soft imperative keeps a senior peer on side; a bare order creates friction.
B The two sentences mean exactly the same and feel the same.
C Because he wants the budget discussed right now.
2. Which form is correct after "would you mind"?
A "Would you mind to keep it brief?" (mind + to + verb)
B "Would you mind keeping it brief?" (mind + -ing)
C "Would you mind keep it brief?" (mind + bare verb)
3. What form follows "shall we" and "why don't we"?
A The -ing form ("shall we moving on").
B The bare verb, with no "to" ("shall we move on").
C "to" + verb ("shall we to move on").

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Soft Imperatives & Steering Language

GRAMMAR

A chair instructs the room constantly — but always as an invitation.

StructureJobExample
Shall we / Why don't we + bare verbpropose the next step"Shall we move on to item two?"
Would you mind + -ingask politely to do / stop something"Would you mind keeping it brief?"
Could we + bare verbredirect gently"Could we come back to that later?"
In the interest of time / Let's move onsteering markers (guard the clock)"In the interest of time, let's move on."
Watch the trap: after would you mind use the -ing form ("mind keeping"), NOT "to". After shall we / why don't we / could we use the bare verb, NO "to" ("shall we start"). PT: o chair instrui a sala o tempo todo, mas sempre em forma de convite. Use "shall we / why don't we + verbo base" para propor ("shall we start?"), "would you mind + -ing" para pedir com educacao ("would you mind keeping it brief?") e "could we + verbo base" para redirecionar ("could we come back to that?"). Cuidado: depois de "would you mind" vem o -ing, NUNCA "to"; depois de "shall we / could we" vem o verbo base, sem "to". Marcadores como "in the interest of time" e "let's move on" protegem o relogio. Mesmo controle, muito menos atrito — a sala segue porque voce convidou, nao porque mandou.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

Propose: "Shall we with the agenda?"
Ask politely: "Would you mind it to two minutes?"
Redirect: "Could we back to that as a sidebar?"
Guard the clock: "In the of time, let's move on."
Bring in: "Why don't we from Carla on this?"

Stage 2: Put the Meeting in Order

Order

Put the jobs of chairing a meeting in the order a skilled chair would use them.

?Bring the quiet people in by name and protect the clock.
?Open with the purpose and a timed agenda.
?Recap the decisions and owners, then adjourn on time.
?Park digressions as a sidebar to keep on topic.
?Confirm everyone knows what was decided before they leave.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

We're here to lock the date and leave with an owner per region.
Shall we move on to the next item?
Would you mind parking that as a sidebar for after?
In the interest of time, could we come back to that later?
Let me recap the decisions and owners before we adjourn.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each chairing situation.

You're chairing a meeting that hasn't started. How do you open?
A "Let's just see where the conversation goes today."
B "We're here to lock the date and leave with an owner per region."
C "I don't really know why we're here, but let's start."
A senior peer keeps digressing. What's the cleanest way to steer?
A "Stop talking about that, it's off-topic."
B "Would you mind parking that as a sidebar for after?"
C "Would you mind to park that for after?"
You want to bring a quiet, well-informed colleague into the discussion. What's best?
A "Does anyone have anything to add? No? Moving on."
B "Why don't we hear from Carla — what's your read?"
C "Shall we to ask someone else for once?"
The meeting is ending. How do you make sure it actually counts?
A "We're out of time — bye, everyone."
B "Let me recap the decisions and owners, then we'll adjourn."
C "We'll figure out what we decided over email later."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Record yourself chairing a real meeting at Brink's from open to close. Open with the purpose, steer one digression into a sidebar with "would you mind" or "could we", bring one quiet person in by name, protect the clock with "in the interest of time", and finish with a recap of decisions and owners before you adjourn. Use at least four of the new words.

Survival Card -- Lesson 17

1We're here to lock the date and leave with an owner per region.
2Why don't we hear from Carla — what's your read?
3Would you mind parking that as a sidebar for after?
4In the interest of time, could we come back to that?
5Let me recap the decisions and owners before we adjourn.
Aula 18 -- Pre-class

Block 5 Review -- The Global Meeting Simulation

Consolidate the whole block in one global cross-regional call. Review the five skills from Lessons 13–17: handle tough questions (hedge, defer, circle back), deliver a setback and reassure (damage control), align two factions on a middle ground (consensus), enlist an ally and sway the room (influence), and chair from open to close. Review words: hedge, defer, circle back, setback, mitigate, reassure, align, middle ground, concede, enlist, sway, steer. Grammar consolidation: indirect questions (statement word order), softening + active voice for bad news, the second conditional "if + past, would + verb" to broker a deal, and soft imperatives "shall we / could we + bare verb".
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The key words from across the block, back in your hands. Tap Listen to hear each one.

Hedge -- to avoid a firm answer when you are not certain yet (PT: evitar uma resposta definitiva)
"I'd hedge on the exact figure until the audit is final."
Defer -- to put off a question or detail until later (PT: adiar / deixar para depois)
"I'll defer the deep numbers and follow up by email."
Circle back -- to return to a topic at a later point (PT: voltar a um assunto depois)
"Good question -- let me circle back on that after the call."
Setback -- a delay or problem that holds back progress (PT: revés / contratempo)
"We hit a setback on the rollout, but it's recoverable."
Mitigate -- to make a problem or its damage less serious (PT: mitigar / reduzir o impacto)
"We added a backup supplier to mitigate the delay."
Align -- to bring teams into agreement on a direction (PT: alinhar)
"We need to align the two regions on one date."
Middle ground -- a compromise both sides can accept (PT: meio-termo)
"Phase one now, phase two in Q3 -- that's our middle ground."
Concede -- to give up a point, often to reach agreement (PT: ceder)
"I'll concede the timeline if you concede the budget."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Hedge
Defer
Circle back
Setback
Mitigate
Align
Middle ground
Concede

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the recap of the global call and answer the questions.

On the global call, four grammar moves did the heavy lifting. When a region pushed him on the numbers, the Growth Manager bought time with an indirect question — "Could you tell me how the figure was reached?" — notice the inside keeps statement order, "how the figure was reached", not "how was the figure reached". When a pilot slipped, he did not hide behind the passive; he softened it and used active voice: "I'm afraid we missed the date, and I own it" — "we missed it", not "mistakes were made". With two factions stuck, he brokered a deal using the second conditional: "If we phased it, would you back it?" — that is if + past, then would + bare verb, for a situation that is still hypothetical. And to keep the room moving he used a soft imperative: "Shall we move on?" — after shall we the verb is bare, with no to. Right tool, right moment — that is the whole block in one call.

1. Which word order is correct inside an indirect question?
A "Could you tell me how was the figure reached?" (inverted)
B "Could you tell me how the figure was reached?" (statement order)
C "Could you tell me how reached the figure was?"
2. Which is the stronger way to deliver bad news?
A "Mistakes were made and the date was missed." (evasive passive)
B "We missed the date, and I own it." (active voice, accountable)
C "The date might have been missed by someone."
3. How is the second conditional formed to broker a deal?
A "If we phase it, will you back it?" (first conditional)
B "If we phased it, would you back it?" (if + past, would + verb)
C "If we would phase it, you backed it."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Block 5 Grammar Map

GRAMMAR

Four structures, one decision in the moment: which one fits the job?

StructureSkillExample
Indirect question (no inversion)handle Q&A, buy time"Could you tell me how it was reached?"
Softener + active voicedeliver a setback, own it"I'm afraid we missed the date."
Second conditional (if + past, would + verb)broker, build consensus"If we phased it, would you back it?"
Soft imperative (shall we / could we + bare verb)influence & chair"Shall we move on for now?"
Watch the traps: an indirect question keeps statement order ("where it is", not "where is it"). Bad news stays in active voice with a softener ("we missed it", not "mistakes were made"). The second conditional is if + past + would + bare verb ("if we phased it, would you back it"). Soft imperatives take the bare verb after "shall we / could we" ("shall we start"). PT: quatro estruturas do bloco, uma decisao no momento. Indirect question NAO inverte ("could you tell me onde esta", e nao "onde esta isso"). Ma noticia em voz ATIVA com amortecedor ("nos perdemos o prazo", nunca "erros foram cometidos"). Segundo condicional = if + passado, would + verbo base ("if we phased it, would you back it"). Soft imperative = verbo base apos "shall we / could we". A habilidade do bloco e escolher a ferramenta certa na hora certa.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

Q&A: "Could you tell me how the figure reached?"
Damage: "I'm afraid missed the date, and I own it."
Consensus: "If we it, would you back the plan?"
Influence: "There's a real win in this for your , so I'd love your backing."
Chair: "Let me the decisions and owners before we adjourn."

Stage 2: Put the Global Call in Order

Order

Put the five skills in the order they came up on Victor's global call.

?Own the setback in active voice and reassure with a plan to mitigate it.
?Open and chair the call with a clear purpose.
?Offer a middle ground and get each side to concede a little.
?Field a tough question: hedge, defer, and circle back.
?Recap the decisions and owners, then adjourn on time.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

That's a fair question, let me circle back with the exact figure.
I'm afraid we hit a setback, and we own it.
If we phased it, would you back the middle ground?
There's a real win in this for you, can I count on your backing?
Let me recap the decisions and owners before we adjourn.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each moment on a global call.

A region fires a number you can't confirm. What's best?
A "It's roughly that, I think, probably." (a guess)
B "Fair question -- I'd hedge for now and circle back with the exact figure."
C "Could you tell me what is the number?" (inverted indirect question)
A milestone slipped and the room is worried. How do you deliver it?
A "Mistakes were made, but it's not really anyone's fault."
B "I'm afraid we hit a setback -- we own it, and here's how we'll mitigate it."
C "Everything is completely fine, ignore the dates." (denial)
Two regions are at a stalemate. What moves them?
A "One of you is just wrong, so let's vote."
B "If we phased it, would you back it? Each side concedes a little for a middle ground."
C "If we would phase it, you will back it." (wrong conditional)
You need backing from a peer who doesn't report to you. What's best?
A "You have to support this because I said so."
B "There's a real win in this for your region -- can I enlist your backing?"
C "It doesn't matter what you think anyway."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Record yourself running a five-minute global call at Brink's that uses all five block skills: open and chair it, field a tough question by hedging and circling back, deliver a setback in active voice and reassure with a plan to mitigate it, broker a middle ground with "if we phased it, would you...", and influence the room by naming the win for them. Close with a recap of decisions and owners. Use at least four of the review words.

Survival Card -- Lesson 18

1That's a fair question, let me circle back with the exact figure.É uma boa pergunta, deixa eu voltar nisso com o número exato.
2I'm afraid we hit a setback, and we own it.Infelizmente tivemos um revés, e assumimos isso.
3If we phased it, would you back the middle ground?Se a gente fizesse por fases, você apoiaria o meio-termo?
4There's a real win in this for you, can I count on your backing?Tem um ganho real nisso para você, posso contar com o seu apoio?
5Let me recap the decisions and owners before we adjourn.Deixa eu recapitular as decisões e os responsáveis antes de encerrarmos.
Aula 19 -- Pre-class

Feedback & Performance Reviews

Open Block 6: run a fair performance review. Give feedback that helps people grow — open with specific recognition, anchor praise in a result with the STAR method (Situation, Task, Action, Result), balance it with one honest, actionable development area, and ask for feedback in return. New vocab: appraisal, feedback, praise, recognition, strengths, development area, accomplishment, underdeliver, actionable, follow-through, raise the bar, commendable. Grammar: reported speech — the backshift. When you report what someone said, the tense steps back (present → past, past → past perfect, will → would, can → could), tell takes a person (told me) while say does not, and time words move (today → that day, tomorrow → the next day): "She told me she was stretched too thin", "He said he would fix it".
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The language of a fair review. Tap Listen to hear each word.

Appraisal -- a formal meeting that evaluates how someone has performed (PT: avaliação de desempenho)
"Her mid-year appraisal is scheduled for Thursday."
Feedback -- information you give someone about their work to help them improve (PT: retorno / feedback)
"Can I give you some feedback on the deck?"
Recognition -- crediting someone openly for a real contribution or result (PT: reconhecimento)
"Public recognition matters more to her than a bonus."
Accomplishment -- a meaningful result a person achieved through their own effort (PT: realização / feito)
"Closing that account was a real accomplishment."
Development area -- a skill or behaviour a person still needs to improve (PT: ponto a desenvolver)
"Time management is his main development area this year."
Underdeliver -- to produce less or worse than was promised or expected (PT: entregar menos do que o esperado)
"He underdelivered on the dashboard, and we need to talk."
Actionable -- clear and specific enough that the person can actually act on it (PT: prático / acionável)
"Make the feedback actionable: tell her exactly what to change."
Commendable -- genuinely deserving of praise (PT: louvável / digno de elogio)
"Her work with the new client has been commendable."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Appraisal
Feedback
Recognition
Accomplishment
Development area
Underdeliver
Actionable
Commendable

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read how a review gets reported up the chain, and answer the questions.

After the mid-year review, the Growth Manager wrote a short note to his director, and that note runs on reported speech. In the meeting Sofia had said, "I am stretched too thin," so in the note he wrote that she said she was stretched too thin — the present am steps back to the past was. She had also said, "I will fix the dashboard," which becomes she said she would fix it — will becomes would. And when she promised, "I finished the proposal," he reported that she said she had finished it — the past simple steps back to the past perfect. Notice the verb choice: he used told when he named the listener ("she told me she needed support") but said when he did not ("she said she was proud of the quarter"). The time words moved too: "I'll send it today" became she said she would send it that day. Get the backshift right and a report sounds precise, not sloppy.

1. "I am stretched too thin" reported becomes:
A "She said she is stretched too thin." (no backshift)
B "She said she was stretched too thin." (present → past)
C "She said she has been stretched too thin."
2. Which verb correctly names the listener?
A "She said me she needed support." (say + person)
B "She told me she needed support." (tell + person)
C "She telled to me she needed support."
3. "I will fix it" reported becomes:
A "He said he will fix it." (will unchanged)
B "He said he would fix it." (will → would)
C "He said he fixed it."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Reported Speech & the Backshift

GRAMMAR

When you report what was said, three things move together.

Direct speechSteps back toReported speech
present "I am ready"past simple"she said she was ready"
past "I finished it"past perfect"he said he had finished it"
will "I will fix it"would"she said she would fix it"
can / mustcould / had to"he said he could / had to"
The three moves: (1) the tense steps back (present→past, past→past perfect, will→would, can→could, must→had to); (2) tell needs a person ("she told me..."), say does not ("she said that..."); (3) time words shift (today→that day, tomorrow→the next day, now→then, yesterday→the day before). PT: ao reportar o que alguem disse, tres coisas mudam juntas. O tempo verbal da um passo atras (presente vira passado, passado vira mais-que-perfeito, will vira would, can vira could). "Tell" pede uma pessoa ("she told me"), "say" nao ("she said that"). E as palavras de tempo mudam (today vira that day, tomorrow vira the next day). Assim "I'll send it tomorrow" vira "she said she would send it the next day".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each reported sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I am proud of this quarter." → She said she proud of that quarter.
"I will raise the bar." → He said he raise the bar.
"I need more support." → She me she needed more support.
"I finished the dashboard." → He said he finished the dashboard.
"I'll send it today." → She said she would send it .

Stage 2: Put the Review in Order

Order

Put the five moves of a fair review in the right order.
