?Name one honest development area and make it actionable.
?Open with specific recognition, not a vague "good job".
?Ask the person for feedback on how you lead.
?Anchor the praise in a result using STAR.
?Report what was said up the chain with the backshift.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I want to start by recognizing your work — it's been commendable.
When it was at risk, you stepped in, and the result was a win.
One development area — let's make it actionable with a follow-through.
She told me she was stretched too thin, so we're rebalancing.
What feedback do you have for me on how I lead the team?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each moment in a review.

You open a review for a strong performer. What's best?
A "So, let's talk about everything you got wrong this year."
B "I want to start by recognizing your client work — it's been commendable."
C "Good job. Anyway, moving on."
How do you make praise concrete with STAR?
A "You're great, honestly, just really great."
B "When the renewal was at risk, you rebuilt the proposal, and we kept the contract."
C "Everyone says nice things about you."
You report to your director what an analyst said: "I'm overloaded." What's correct?
A "He said he is overloaded." (no backshift)
B "He told me he was overloaded." (backshift + tell)
C "He said me he is overloaded."
A development area exists but you want it to help, not hurt. What's best?
A "You're just bad at dashboards." (vague, personal)
B "The dashboard underdelivered — let's make it actionable with a template and a check-in."
C "Whatever, it's fine, don't worry about it." (no follow-through)

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Record yourself giving a mid-year review to one real person on your team at Brink's. Open with specific recognition anchored in STAR (situation, task, action, result), name one actionable development area with a follow-through you'd both own, ask them for feedback on how you lead, and then report two things they said using reported speech with the backshift — "she said she was...", "he told me he would...". Use at least four of the new words.

Survival Card -- Lesson 19

1I want to start by recognizing your work — it's been commendable.Quero começar reconhecendo o seu trabalho — foi louvável.
2When it was at risk, you stepped in, and the result was a win.Quando estava em risco, você entrou em ação, e o resultado foi uma vitória.
3One development area — let's make it actionable with a follow-through.Um ponto a desenvolver — vamos torná-lo prático, com um próximo passo combinado.
4She told me she was stretched too thin, so we're rebalancing.Ela me disse que estava sobrecarregada, então estamos reequilibrando.
5What feedback do you have for me on how I lead the team?Que feedback você tem para mim sobre como eu lidero a equipe?
Aula 20 -- Pre-class

Coaching & Development Conversations

Close Block 6 and the program: run a coaching conversation that grows someone between reviews. Coach by asking, not telling, so the person owns the plan — lead with open questions that start with what or how ("What would success look like?", "What's the one roadblock?"), and build the growth plan as cause and effect with the zero conditional. New vocab: coaching, mentor, potential, growth mindset, stretch goal, ownership, reflect, breakthrough, empower, unlock, self-aware, roadblock. Grammar: open coaching questions vs closed yes/no questions, and the zero conditional — if + present simple, present simple ("If you block two hours a day, the deep work gets done") for reliable cause and effect, distinct from the first conditional (if + present, will) for one specific future result.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The language of a coaching conversation. Tap Listen to hear each word.

Coaching -- helping someone improve by asking questions, not by giving the answers (PT: coaching / orientar fazendo perguntas)
"Good coaching is mostly listening and asking the next question."
Mentor -- an experienced person who guides someone less experienced over time (PT: mentor)
"He became a mentor to two junior analysts."
Potential -- ability someone has that is not fully used or developed yet (PT: potencial)
"She has the potential to lead the whole region."
Growth mindset -- the belief that ability can be developed through effort, not fixed (PT: mentalidade de crescimento)
"A growth mindset turns a setback into a lesson."
Stretch goal -- an ambitious target that pushes a person beyond their current level (PT: meta desafiadora)
"We set a stretch goal: own a full region by year-end."
Ownership -- taking full personal responsibility for a task, plan, or result (PT: senso de dono / responsabilidade)
"Once she took ownership of the plan, the energy changed."
Roadblock -- something that is stopping or slowing someone's progress (PT: obstáculo / empecilho)
"What's the one roadblock between you and that goal?"
Breakthrough -- a sudden, important step forward after a period of difficulty (PT: avanço decisivo)
"The breakthrough came when she stopped waiting for permission."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Coaching
Mentor
Potential
Stretch goal
Ownership
Roadblock
Breakthrough
Growth mindset

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read how a coach builds a growth plan, and answer the questions.

A good coaching conversation runs on two tools. The first is the open question. Instead of asking "Are you stuck?", which only gets a yes or a no and ends the conversation, the coach asks "What's the one roadblock in the way?" — an open question that starts with what or how and makes the person think. The second tool is the zero conditional, which maps the growth plan as reliable cause and effect: "If you block two hours each morning, the deep work gets done." Notice the shape — if + present simple, then present simple; there is no will, because this is a general truth about how things work, not a single future prediction. Compare the first conditional, which you already know: "If you block tomorrow morning, you will finish the deck" describes one specific future result. The zero conditional ("if you block your mornings, the work gets done") describes a system that always works. A coach asks open questions, then maps the plan with zero conditionals — and lets the person own it.

1. Which is the open coaching question?
A "Are you stuck?" (yes/no — closed)
B "What's the one roadblock in the way?" (open)
C "Do you need help?" (yes/no — closed)
2. The zero conditional for a growth plan is:
A "If you will block your mornings, the work gets done." (will after if)
B "If you block your mornings, the work gets done." (if + present, present)
C "If you blocked your mornings, the work would get done."
3. What makes the zero conditional different from the first conditional?
A Nothing — they are the same.
B Zero (if + present, present) = a general truth/system; first (if + present, will) = one future result.
C The zero conditional always uses "would".

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Open Questions & the Zero Conditional

GRAMMAR

Two tools carry a coaching conversation.

ToolShapeExample
Open questionWhat / How + clause"What would success look like?"
Closed questionDo / Are / Can + ... (yes/no)"Are you on track?" — avoid in coaching
Zero conditionalIf + present, present"If you block mornings, the work gets done."
vs 1st conditionalIf + present, will"If you block tomorrow, you will finish it."
The two tools: (1) ask open questions (what/how/why) that can't be answered yes or no — a closed question (do/are/can) ends the conversation; (2) map the plan with the zero conditional: if + present simple, present simple, for reliable cause and effect, never will after if. PT: duas ferramentas conduzem uma conversa de coaching. Faça perguntas ABERTAS (what/how/why), que não podem ser respondidas com sim/não — uma pergunta fechada (do/are/can) encerra a conversa. E mapeie o plano com o ZERO CONDITIONAL: if + presente, presente, para causa e efeito que sempre vale ("se você bloqueia as manhãs, o trabalho sai"). Nunca use will logo depois de if. Compare com o first conditional (if + presente, will), que é para um resultado futuro específico.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each line with the correct word or form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

Open it up: " would success look like for you?"
Open it up: " will you know you're making progress?"
Zero conditional: "If you two hours a day, the deep work gets done."
Zero conditional: "If you ask for feedback early, the surprises ."
Hand over the step: "So what's one step you could take this ?"

Stage 2: Put the Session in Order

Order

Put the four moves of a coaching session in the right order.

?Map the plan as cause and effect with a zero conditional.
?Open with a forward-looking question about success.
?Hand over ownership of one concrete step this week.
?Explore the roadblock with another open question.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

What would success look like for you in six months?
What's the one roadblock getting in the way right now?
If you block two hours each morning, the deep work gets done.
So what's one step you could take this week?
That's the breakthrough — and it's yours, not mine.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each moment in a coaching session.

You want a team member to reflect on their goal. What's best?
A "Do you want to grow?" (yes/no — closed)
B "What would success look like for you in a year?" (open)
C "Here's exactly what you should do." (telling, not coaching)
You want to map a growth plan as cause and effect. What's correct?
A "If you will protect your mornings, the work gets done." (will after if)
B "If you protect your mornings, the deep work gets done." (zero conditional)
C "If you protected your mornings, the work would get done." (not a general truth)
The person pauses and looks unsure. What does a good coach do?
A Jump in immediately with the answer. (rescuing, not coaching)
B Hold the silence and let them think — "Take your time. What comes to mind?"
C Change the subject to avoid the awkward pause.
You want the person to own the next step. What's best?
A "I'll set up the plan and send it to you." (you own it, not them)
B "So what's one step you could take this week to own it?" (hands over ownership)
C "Just do whatever you think." (vague, no commitment)

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Record yourself coaching one real person on your team at Brink's toward a stretch goal. Open with a forward-looking open question, explore one roadblock with another open question, map at least two cause-and-effect steps with the zero conditional (if + present, present), and end by asking them to commit to one step they own. Use at least four of the new words.

Survival Card -- Lesson 20

1What would success look like for you in six months?Como seria o sucesso para você daqui a seis meses?
2What's the one roadblock getting in the way right now?Qual é o principal obstáculo no caminho agora?
3If you block two hours each morning, the deep work gets done.Se você reserva duas horas toda manhã, o trabalho de foco sai.
4So what's one step you could take this week?Então, qual é um passo que você poderia dar esta semana?
5That's the breakthrough — and it's yours, not mine.Esse é o avanço — e ele é seu, não meu.

IN CLASS -- Selecione a Aula

01
Diagnostic + Personal Baseline
Who Is Victor Paschotto at Brinks? — Present Perfect vs Simple Past — 31 slides
02
Owning the Room
Presenting Growth Data — Third Conditional + Hedging — 32 slides
03
Navigating Tough Conversations
Pushback & Diplomatic Disagreement — Modal Perfects — 32 slides
04
Reporting What Was Said
Meetings & Follow-Up — Reported Speech + Reporting Verbs — 32 slides
05
Emails That Move
Requests, Follow-Ups & Escalations — Formal Register, Hedging & Conditionals — 33 slides
06
The Mid-Block Review
Checkpoint — Lessons 1–5 Consolidated: Achievements, What-Ifs, Speculation, Debriefs & Register — 33 slides
07
Pitching with Impact
Persuasion & Influence: sell a growth initiative with the second conditional and a sharp value proposition — 33 slides
08
Negotiating the Deal
Holding your ground & trading concessions: close a deal with first-conditional trades, negotiation connectors and a calm, firm tone — 33 slides
09
Cross-Cultural Communication
Reading the room across business cultures: interpret signals with modals of deduction (might, must, can't), adapt your directness, and protect the relationship — 33 slides
10
Difficult Conversations
Handling tension with care: open a hard talk without triggering defensiveness, soften the message with tentative language, stay candid but constructive, and protect the relationship — 33 slides
11
Presenting to the Board
Tell the growth story to the board: set the backdrop, build to a turning point with narrative tenses, switch to the dramatic present for the key moment, signpost every step, and land one clear takeaway — 33 slides
12
The Stakeholder Meeting
Checkpoint for Blocks 7–11: run a full mock stakeholder meeting that pulls together pitching with the second conditional, negotiating with the first conditional, reading the room with modals of deduction, handling tension with tentative language, and telling the story with narrative tenses and signposting — 33 slides
13
Handling Q&A
Field tough questions under pressure: read a loaded question, buy a moment to think, hedge an answer you are not certain about, clarify with indirect questions, and defer cleanly when you cannot commit — with question forms, hedging language, and time-buying phrases — 33 slides
14
Damage Control
Deliver bad news with an empathetic structure and steady the room: lead in with a softening opener, state the setback in active voice, take ownership without over-apologizing, then reassure with a concrete forward plan — using will for firm promises, going to / be + -ing for arranged steps, and the first conditional for contingencies — 33 slides
15
Building Consensus
Align a divided room without steamrolling anyone: acknowledge both sides fairly, name the common ground everyone shares, propose a way forward with hypothetical language (what if we / suppose we / I'd suggest), build on others' points instead of overriding them, and close by checking everyone can live with the decision — turning a stalemate into a workable win-win — 33 slides
16
Influencing Without Authority
Get buy-in from people who do not report to you: find the win for them, get them to act through framing rather than orders, enlist an ally before the meeting, and rally support around a shared goal — using causative structures (get someone to / have someone do) and purpose clauses (so that / in order to) to move the org without pulling rank — 33 slides
17
Leading the Room
Chair and steer a meeting from start to finish: open with a clear purpose and agenda, manage the floor by bringing quiet people in and cutting dominators off politely, park digressions and sidebars, keep to time, drive the room to a decision, and close with a recap and owners — using chairing language: soft imperatives (shall we / why don't we + bare verb), polite control (would you mind + -ing, could we + verb), and steering markers (let's move on, can we come back to that, in the interest of time) — 33 slides
18
Block 5 Review — The Global Meeting
Put the whole block together in one global meeting simulation: field tough questions and hedge (Q&A), deliver a setback and reassure (damage control), align two factions on a middle ground (consensus), enlist an ally and sway the room (influence), and chair from open to close (leading the room) — consolidating the block's structures: indirect questions, softening language, hypotheticals, and soft imperatives — 33 slides
19
Feedback & Performance Reviews
Open Block 6: give and take feedback in a performance review. Structure feedback with the STAR method (Situation, Task, Action, Result), balance praise with a development area, and report what was said with reported speech — backshifting tense and shifting pronouns and time words ("she said the report had been late", "he told me he would fix it"). New vocab: appraisal, feedback, praise, recognition, strengths, development area, accomplishment, underdeliver, actionable, follow-through, raise the bar, commendable — 33 slides
20
Coaching & Development Conversations
Close Block 6 and the program: run a coaching conversation that grows someone between reviews. Lead with open coaching questions instead of giving answers ("What would success look like?", "What is one step you could take this week?"), and use the zero conditional to map cause and effect in a growth plan ("If you block two hours a day, the deep work gets done"). New vocab: coaching, mentor, potential, growth mindset, stretch goal, ownership, reflect, breakthrough, empower, unlock, self-aware, roadblock — 33 slides

Atividades Complementares

Materiais extras para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula. Marque como concluído após assistir/ouvir.

Lesson 1 — Diagnostic + Personal Baseline

Series
Industry (HBO) -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Sesh"

Young professionals navigating the corporate world at a top London investment bank. Observe how they introduce themselves to senior colleagues, describe their roles, and use corporate vocabulary under pressure. Direct connection to Lesson 1: professional self-introductions, navigating hierarchy, and making a first impression in a multinational environment.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Note 3 expressions the characters use when describing their roles or responsibilities to senior staff.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — How to Scale a Start-Up

Harvard Business School's Jeffrey Rayport breaks down why fast-growing companies stall at the scale-up phase and what separates those that scale successfully. Rich vocabulary about growth strategy, stakeholders, and staying profitable at scale.

Tip: listen to the first 25 minutes. Write down 5 expressions related to growth strategy that you hear.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
TED Talk -- Simon Sinek: "How Great Leaders Inspire Action"

Simon Sinek's iconic talk about the "Golden Circle" and why great leaders start with WHY. Relevant for Victor's growth role: how to articulate vision and strategy to stakeholders. Connection to the lesson: professional language for describing purpose, motivating teams, and communicating results to leadership.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Practice repeating 3 phrases Sinek uses when explaining how leaders communicate their vision.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 2 — Owning the Room -- Presenting Growth Data

Series
Billions (Showtime) -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

High-stakes financial world where hedge fund managers present data to stakeholders, defend their strategies, and use persuasive language under pressure. Pay attention to how characters frame numbers, use hedging language ("it would appear"), and make compelling cases to convince skeptical counterparts. Direct connection to Lesson 2: presenting data to a skeptical VP, using numbers persuasively, and defending performance with confidence.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 expressions the characters use when presenting financial data or defending a position to a superior.

Disponível no streaming: Paramount+

Podcast
Freakonomics Radio — The Folly of Prediction

Humans love to predict the future but are terrible at it, and because bad forecasts are rarely punished, that won't change. The episode explores why expert predictions go wrong and how to treat uncertain forecasts with humility.

Tip: listen to the first 30 minutes. Note how the speakers use words like "roughly," "it appears," and "the data suggests" when discussing uncertain outcomes.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
TED Talk -- Hans Rosling: "The best stats you've ever seen"

Hans Rosling's legendary presentation transforms dry global health data into a captivating story using visualization and storytelling. He demonstrates how to present numbers in a way that is engaging, persuasive, and clear to any audience. Direct connection to Lesson 2: how to "own the room" with data, make statistics accessible to non-technical stakeholders, and turn a data presentation into a compelling narrative.

Tip: watch the full talk (20 min). Notice how Rosling uses simple language to explain data, makes comparisons to benchmarks, and keeps the audience engaged. Practice repeating 3 of his key phrases about data trends.

Watch on TED ↗

Lesson 3 — Navigating Tough Conversations -- Pushback, Objections, and Diplomatic Disagreement

Series
Succession (HBO) -- Season 1, Episode 3: "Lifeboats"

This episode features intense boardroom negotiations where family members and executives push back on each other's strategies, escalate conflicts, and attempt to leverage their positions diplomatically. Pay attention to how characters disagree without directly saying "you are wrong," how they use hedging language to soften their positions, and how they read between the lines of what others say. Direct connection to Lesson 3: diplomatic disagreement, navigating pushback from powerful stakeholders, and using language strategically in high-stakes corporate conflict.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 phrases characters use when they disagree with someone more powerful than them. Notice how they acknowledge the other person's point before presenting their own.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
Negotiate Anything (Kwame Christian) — The Hidden Cost of Unresolved Disagreements

A negotiation masterclass with Kwame Christian and three mediators on handling disagreement and pushback when emotions run high — how to stay calm and keep a professional conversation productive.

Tip: listen to the first 25 minutes. Note how Kwame uses phrases like "I understand your concern" and "What I would suggest is..." to disagree respectfully. Practice repeating his key phrases out loud.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
Chris Voss -- "Never Split the Difference" | Talks at Google

Former FBI hostage negotiator Chris Voss shares his most effective negotiation tactics, including tactical empathy, mirroring, and calibrated questions. He demonstrates how to disagree without creating conflict, how to read between the lines of what people say, and how to navigate high-pressure conversations where the stakes are enormous. His approach to "no-oriented questions" is particularly relevant for corporate pushback situations. Direct connection to Lesson 3: using diplomatic language in negotiations, leveraging emotional intelligence in conflict, and navigating conversations where the other party has more power.

Tip: watch the full talk (45 min). Focus on the section about "tactical empathy" and how Voss phrases disagreements. Practice his technique of repeating the last 1-3 words of what someone says to show you are listening before you push back.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — Reporting What Was Said -- Meetings, Briefings, and Follow-Up Communication

Series
The Morning Show (Apple TV+) -- Season 1, Episode 2: "A Seat at the Table"

This episode is packed with newsroom briefings, urgent debriefs, and characters reporting what was said behind closed doors. Pay close attention to how producers and anchors relay information from meetings they attended: "She confirmed that the network would move forward," "He warned us that the story was about to break." The corporate dynamics at UBA mirror multinational boardrooms, with people reporting conversations, denying involvement, and circulating information strategically. Direct connection to Lesson 4: reported speech in high-pressure corporate environments, the difference between what was said and how it gets reported, and how reporting verbs like "warned," "denied," and "insisted" carry different emotional weight.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 5 sentences where a character reports what someone else said. Notice which reporting verbs they use and whether the tense shifts back.

Disponível no streaming: Apple TV+

Podcast
How I Built This with Guy Raz — Airbnb: Joe Gebbia

A chance encounter with a stranger gave Joe Gebbia an idea to help pay his rent, which grew into Airbnb. He recounts the early days, investor meetings, and conversations with co-founders during critical moments.

Tip: listen to the first 30 minutes. Every time Joe reports what someone told him, pause and identify: (1) the reporting verb he used, (2) the tense backshift, and (3) how the tone of the reporting verb changes the feeling of the story.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
Write Professional Emails in English | Step-by-Step

Lucy breaks down the structure and language of professional follow-up emails after meetings. She covers key phrases like "As discussed in our meeting," "To confirm what was agreed," and "Following up on the points we outlined." The video includes real email templates and demonstrates how to report meeting decisions in writing, using the exact grammar structures from Lesson 4. She also addresses common mistakes like mixing direct and reported speech in the same email, and how to choose the right reporting verb depending on the formality of the situation. Direct connection to Lesson 4: writing follow-up emails that use reported speech correctly, choosing between "said," "mentioned," "confirmed," and "warned" for different contexts, and structuring a professional debrief that outlines key decisions and next steps.

Tip: watch the full video (15 min). After watching, write a follow-up email about an imaginary Brinks meeting using at least 3 reporting verbs and correct tense backshifts. Compare your email with the templates in the video.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 5 — Emails That Move -- Requests, Follow-Ups, and Escalations

Series
Suits (Netflix) -- Season 2, Episode 5: "Break Point"

The lawyers at Pearson Hardman live and die by their correspondence. This episode is a masterclass in register: watch how Harvey and Jessica shift from warm and collegial to cold and formal depending on who the recipient is and what is at stake. Notice the hedged requests ("I would appreciate it if you would reconsider"), the tactful escalations, and the way a single carefully worded email can move a negotiation. Direct connection to Lesson 5: the difference between a blunt demand and a diplomatic request, how professionals escalate without burning a relationship, and why concise, pertinent messages win.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 5 moments where a character makes a request or an escalation. Mark each one as Direct, Neutral, or Formal register.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Avoiding Miscommunication in a Digital World

Communication expert Nick Morgan explains why email, texting, and Slack often misfire, and how to write clear, concise messages that get read and get faster replies — including how to make a request without ambiguity.

Tip: listen once for the main ideas, then again with a notepad. Write down 3 techniques you can apply to your next real email at Brinks this week.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
From Informal to Formal Email | Business English Lesson

A practical walkthrough of real email templates at three levels of formality. Christina shows how to soften a request with hedging language, how to follow up without sounding impatient, and how to escalate using conditional sentences. She contrasts direct phrases ("Send this now") with formal alternatives ("I would appreciate it if you could send this by..."), exactly the register ladder from Lesson 5. She also covers common mistakes Brazilian professionals make, such as being too direct or translating Portuguese politeness word for word.

Tip: watch the full video (about 12 min). Then rewrite one blunt request into a neutral version and a formal version. Read all three out loud and feel the difference in tone.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 6 — The Mid-Block Review -- Putting Lessons 1 to 5 Together

Series
Industry (HBO Max) -- Season 2, Episode 1: "Daybreak"

The young bankers at Pierpoint return for performance reviews, and every conversation is a live exam in the five tools from this block. Watch how they present achievements ("I have closed three accounts this quarter"), explain misses ("if I had flagged the risk earlier..."), speculate about a deal that went wrong ("the client must have changed his mind"), debrief what a managing director told them, and ask for support in carefully hedged language. Direct connection to Lesson 6: it shows the same review skills Victor needs, under pressure, in a real corporate setting.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Pause five times and label each line with one of the five tools: achievement, unreal past, speculation, debrief, or register.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — How to Develop Your Executive Presence

Communication coach Laura Sicola explains how to present results with authority, field tough questions, and stay composed off the cuff — the core skills for owning a quarterly review and reporting misses without being defensive.

Tip: listen once for structure, then draft your own three-line review opener: one achievement, one honest setback, one next step. Read it out loud twice.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Learn ALL 16 TENSES Easily in under 30 Minutes - Present, Past, Future, Conditional

A compact review of the exact structures consolidated in Lesson 6. The presenters contrast present perfect with past simple, walk through second and third conditionals side by side, and show how reported speech backshifts tenses. They use clear workplace examples and flag the most common mistakes Brazilian professionals make, such as adding a finished time to the present perfect or putting "would" in the if-clause.

Tip: watch with a notebook. Every time a structure appears, write one sentence about your own work using the same pattern.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 7 — Pitching with Impact -- Persuasion, Influence & the Second Conditional

Series
Shark Tank (ABC) -- any episode of Season 10

Every founder who walks in does exactly what you practiced: open with a value proposition, sell a hypothetical future, and ask for a specific investment. Listen for the hooks ("if you backed us, we would corner this market") and watch how the investors push back on cost and risk -- and how the strongest founders reframe instead of getting defensive. A masterclass in pitching under pressure.

Tip: pause after each pitch and write the founder's value proposition in one sentence, plus one second-conditional line they used ("If we... we would...").

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Getting Buy-In for Your Next Big Idea

Ellen Bailey of Harvard Business Publishing breaks down why some ideas win backing: how to tailor a pitch, frame the value proposition, and use persuasion tactics to get stakeholders on board. Directly matches the card's pitching theme.

Tip: after listening, draft a three-line pitch for one real idea at Brinks: the value proposition, one second-conditional hook, and one small ask.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Second Conditional Sentences + Examples | English Grammar Lesson

A clear, compact lesson on the exact structure from this class: if + past simple, would + base verb. The presenter shows how the second conditional describes imagined or unlikely situations, contrasts it with the first and third conditionals, and flags the most common error Brazilian professionals make -- putting "would" inside the if-clause.

Tip: watch with a notebook. After each example, write one sentence pitching a real idea at work using the same "If we... we would..." pattern.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 8 — Negotiating the Deal -- Holding Your Ground & Trading Concessions

Series
Suits (USA Network) -- any negotiation scene with Harvey Specter

Harvey rarely raises his voice, yet he wins almost every negotiation. Watch how he opens from common ground, names his non-negotiable calmly, and never gives a concession without taking one back. Notice the trade-offs: every time he gives something, he gets something. It is exactly the give-and-take you practiced in class, played out under pressure.

Tip: pause after each scene and write one trade you saw ("If X, then Y"), plus the one point where the character held firm.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
Negotiate Anything (Kwame Christian) — Split the Pie: A Radical New Way to Negotiate (with Barry J. Nalebuff)

Kwame Christian talks with economist Barry Nalebuff about his 'Split the Pie' method — how to map common ground and trade value fairly instead of simply giving concessions away.

Tip: after listening, draft three lines for one real deal at Brinks: your common ground, one first-conditional trade, and your single non-negotiable.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
A review of conditionals: BBC English Class

A clear, compact lesson on the exact structures from this class: if + present simple, will + base verb, plus the connectors unless, as long as and provided that. The presenter shows how each one sets a single condition, and flags the most common error -- putting "will" inside the if-clause, or adding a second negative after "unless".

Tip: watch with a notebook. After each example, write one real trade you could offer a supplier using the same "If you... we will..." pattern.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 9 — Cross-Cultural Communication -- Reading the Room Across Business Cultures

Series
The Diplomat (Netflix) -- any episode with a high-stakes cross-cultural meeting

The lead constantly reads rooms full of people from different countries and ranks. Watch how she senses the mood, adapts her directness, and protects relationships when a blunt word could cause real damage. Notice how often she reads a signal out loud in her head before she reacts -- "they might be testing us", "that can't be a coincidence" -- exactly the deduction you practiced in class.

Tip: pause after a tense scene and write one reading using might, must, or can't, plus how she adapted her tone.

Disponível no streaming: Netflix

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — How Authority and Decision-Making Differ Across Cultures

INSEAD professor Erin Meyer, author of The Culture Map, explains how hierarchy, directness, and decision-making vary from country to country and how leaders adapt when running global teams.

Tip: after listening, write three lines for one real call at Brinks: a signal you might misread, how you would check it, and how you would build rapport first.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Erin Meyer -- "The Culture Map" (author talk on cross-cultural communication)

A clear, example-rich talk on how cultures differ in directness, trust, hierarchy and how they handle disagreement. Meyer shows why the same behavior reads as polite in one place and rude in another, and how to adapt without losing yourself. It maps perfectly onto the read-and-adapt skill from this class.

Tip: watch with a notebook. For each culture she describes, write one sentence reading a likely signal with might, must, or can't.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 10 — Difficult Conversations -- Handling Tension with Care

Series
Ted Lasso (Apple TV+) -- any episode with a one-to-one between a coach and a struggling player

Ted is a master of the difficult conversation: he opens with empathy, stays candid about the problem, and almost never puts the other person on the defensive. Watch how he handles a tense moment with a player or a colleague — he names the issue honestly but keeps it constructive, defuses the tension, and protects the relationship every time.

Tip: pause after a hard conversation and write how Ted opened it. Could you rewrite his first line with "I was hoping..." or an I-statement?

Disponível no streaming: Apple TV+

Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — The Science of Productive Conflict

Adam Grant explains how the best teams disagree well: why people get defensive, how empathy defuses tension, and how to turn conflict into better decisions instead of avoiding it.

Tip: after listening, draft three lines for one real conversation: a tentative opener, one candid sentence about the issue, and a closing line to clear the air.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
Adar Cohen -- "3 ways to lead the toughest conversations" (TED)

A short, practical TED talk on how to walk into the conversations everyone avoids: ask honest questions, share the real reason, and stay in the room when it gets hard. It maps perfectly onto the open-with-care, stay-candid skill from this class.

Tip: watch with a notebook. For one conversation you've been avoiding, write the first question you would ask to open it without an accusation.

Watch on TED ↗

Lesson 11 — Presenting to the Board -- Slides & Stories

Series
Mad Men (AMC) -- any episode with a Don Draper client pitch (e.g. "The Wheel", S1E13)

Don Draper is a master of the presentation as a story: he sets a backdrop, builds to one turning point, and lands a single takeaway that the room never forgets. Watch how he headlines the idea, walks the client through it, and switches into a vivid, present-tense moment to make them feel it — exactly the craft from this class.

Tip: pause after a pitch and write down the storyline in three lines — backdrop, turning point, takeaway. Notice where he switches to the present.

Disponível no streaming: Disney+

Podcast
Think Fast, Talk Smart — Telling Good Stories: How to Use the Elements of Narrative to Keep Listeners Engaged

Matt Abrahams breaks down the building blocks of narrative — goal, structure, and movable scenes — so your message is logical for the audience and keeps them engaged. A concrete take on how to structure a message that lands.

Tip: after listening, draft three signposting lines for a real presentation: an opening headline, one "let me drill down" transition, and a closing takeaway.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Nancy Duarte -- "The secret structure of great talks" (TEDxEast)

Nancy Duarte shows that the best presentations are built like stories: they move between "what is" and "what could be", build to a turning point, and end on one memorable takeaway. A clear, practical map for turning a board deck into a compelling storyline.

Tip: watch with a notebook and sketch the backdrop, turning point, and takeaway of one presentation you have to give at work.

Watch on TED ↗

Lesson 12 — The Stakeholder Meeting -- Review & Mock Meeting

Series
Succession (HBO) -- any boardroom or shareholder-meeting scene (e.g. S2 "Argestes" or the board votes)

Succession is a masterclass in the stakeholder meeting: characters pitch with imagined scenarios, negotiate real offers with conditions, read the room before they speak, and use careful, tentative language when the tension peaks. Watch one meeting and try to spot all five moves from this class.

Tip: pause whenever someone makes an ask and decide — was that a pitch (would) or a real offer (will)? Note one line that reads the room.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — To Negotiate Better, Start with Yourself

William Ury, co-author of Getting to Yes and co-founder of Harvard's Program on Negotiation, shares methods for negotiating better outcomes — arguing the biggest obstacle is often yourself, not the other side.

Tip: after listening, write one line for each of the five moves about a real meeting you have coming up — story, pitch, deal, read, and care.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Harvard Business Review -- "How to Negotiate" / William Ury on getting to agreement

William Ury, co-author of "Getting to Yes", shows how to move a room from pushback to agreement: find common ground, make conditional offers, and stay calm under tension. A clear, practical bridge between the negotiation block and the full stakeholder meeting.

Tip: watch with a notebook and draft one conditional offer (if you approve… we will…) with two milestones for a real ask at work.

Watch on TED ↗

Lesson 13 — Handling Q&A -- Tough Questions Under Pressure

Series
The Newsroom (HBO) -- any press-conference or on-air Q&A scene, or a White House press briefing on YouTube

Watch a spokesperson field hostile, loaded questions live. Notice how the strong ones buy a second, clarify what is really being asked, hedge what they cannot confirm, and defer cleanly — while the weak ones answer off the cuff and overcommit.

Tip: pause after each tough question and decide which tool you would use — buy time, clarify, hedge, or defer — before they answer.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — How to Develop Your Executive Presence

Cognitive linguist and executive coach Laura Sicola breaks down executive presence: how to handle loaded questions, buy a moment to think, and speak with authority off the cuff — even remotely.

Tip: write down one go-to phrase for each tool — buy time, clarify, hedge, defer — that you would actually say at Brink's.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Think Fast. Talk Smart | Matt Abrahams | TEDxMontaVistaHighSchool

A short, practical guide to handling hard questions: how to buy time without stalling, ask clarifying questions, and use qualifiers so you never overcommit. A clear bridge between presenting (Block 3) and fielding the questions that follow.

Tip: watch with a notebook and draft one indirect clarifying question and one hedged answer for a real ask you expect at work.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 — Damage Control -- Delivering Bad News & Reassuring Stakeholders

Series
Chernobyl (HBO) -- the crisis-briefing scenes, or any corporate-apology / product-recall press conference on YouTube

Watch leaders deliver genuinely bad news under pressure. Notice who stays transparent, owns the miss in active voice, and offers a concrete plan — versus who hides behind passive blame-shifting ("mistakes were made") and over-apology. The contrast is the whole lesson.

Tip: pause after each delivery and label the moves — lead in, own it, reassure — and spot any passive blame-shift.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Why Many Companies Get Layoffs Wrong

Harvard's Sandra Sucher explains how leaders should deliver hard news like layoffs: with transparency, advance notice, accountability, and support — a case study in accountable leadership under pressure.

Tip: write one go-to line for each move — lead in, own it, will-promise, arranged step, contingency — that you would actually say.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Six Keys to Delivering Bad News

A short, practical guide to breaking bad news well: lead in honestly, own the result without over-apologizing, and reassure with future commitments — will for promises, arranged steps, and if-contingencies. A clean bridge from fielding tough questions (Block 4, Lesson 13) to steadying the room after the answer is bad.

Tip: draft one bad-news script for a real situation you expect at work, then read it aloud and check each move.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 15 — Building Consensus -- Aligning a Divided Room

Series
12 Angry Men (the classic film), or any boardroom-deadlock scene in Succession / Industry

Watch a divided room get aligned in real time. Notice who acknowledges the other side first, who names the common ground, and who proposes tentatively versus who just bullies for a vote. The person who turns a stalemate into a yes is doing exactly what this class teaches.

Tip: pause whenever someone unblocks the room and label the move — acknowledge, converge, propose, build on, confirm.

Disponível nos streamings: Prime Video e Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Learn to Disagree More Effectively

Harvard Kennedy School's Julia Minson shares research on productive disagreement: modeling receptiveness, making both sides feel heard, and keeping the conversation going instead of trying to win — the heart of building consensus.

Try one 'receptiveness' phrase from the episode next time you disagree with a colleague.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Harvard Business Review / TED -- "How to Build Consensus" or "Getting to Yes" negotiation breakdowns

A short, practical guide to aligning a divided room: acknowledge both positions, find the common ground, propose with tentative language, and check for genuine agreement. A clean bridge from delivering bad news (Lesson 14) to bringing two factions to a yes everyone owns.

Tip: draft a four-move alignment script for a real split decision you expect at work, then read it aloud and check each move.

Watch on TED ↗

Lesson 16 — Influencing Without Authority -- Getting Buy-In Across the Org

Series
Suits or Industry — any scene where someone gets a peer or rival to act without any power over them

Watch how the best operators move people they cannot order. Notice who leads with the other person's win, who frames a request so the other side feels it was their idea, and who lines up an ally before the room rather than walking in cold. That is influence without authority in action.

Tip: pause whenever someone wins a peer over and label the move — find the win, frame, enlist, rally, reciprocity.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix, Max e Prime Video

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Mastering the Art of Persuasion

Wharton professor Jonah Berger explains why pushing people to change backfires and how to move those you don't manage — leading with their win and letting them talk themselves into the plan. Core skills for influence without authority.

Note his reasons pushing fails, then reframe one request as the other person's win.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Harvard Business Review / TED — "How to Influence Without Authority" or "The Science of Persuasion" (Cialdini)

A short, practical guide to moving the org when you can't pull rank: find the other side's win, frame so they own it, enlist an advocate, and use reciprocity. A clean bridge from building consensus inside your own room (Lesson 15) to winning over peers across the whole org.

Tip: draft a five-move influence script for a real cross-functional ask you expect at work, then read it aloud and check each move.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 17 — Leading the Room -- Chairing & Steering a Meeting

Series
The West Wing / Succession — any scene where one person chairs a tense, high-stakes meeting

Watch how a strong chair opens with a clear purpose, cuts dominators off without insulting them, pulls the quiet expert in, and forces the room to a decision before time runs out. Notice the soft imperatives — "shall we", "would you mind", "could we" — that steer without barking orders.

Tip: pause whenever the chair redirects the room and label the move — open, bring in, park, protect the clock, close.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Supercharge Your One-on-One Meetings

Experts unpack how to facilitate a meeting well: own the purpose and the clock, run the conversation deliberately, keep it on track, and never let it drift — applied to the highest-leverage meeting, the one-on-one.

Listen for the facilitation moves that keep a meeting focused, and try one this week.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Harvard Business Review / TED — "How to Run a Meeting" or "How to Save the World (or at Least Yourself) from Bad Meetings"

A short, practical guide to chairing: open with a stated purpose, time-box the agenda, steer the floor, and close with decisions and owners. A clean bridge from winning peers over without authority (Lesson 16) to running the very room you brought them into.

Tip: draft a five-line run sheet for a real meeting you will chair — one line per job — then read it aloud and check each soft imperative.

Watch on TED ↗

Lesson 18 — Block 5 Review -- The Global Meeting Simulation

Series
Succession / Industry — any high-stakes meeting where one person fields hard questions, owns a setback, brokers a deal, and runs the room

Watch a single scene with all five block skills in play: someone deflects a loaded question, delivers bad news (well or badly), pushes a compromise, wins backing from a peer, and chairs to a decision. The whole block, in one room.

Tip: pause at each turn and label the skill — Q&A, damage control, consensus, influence, or chair.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — When Leading a Global Team, Don't Leave Connection to Chance

Harvard Business School professor Tsedal Neeley explains why globally distributed teams break down when leaders leave connection to chance, and how to structure unstructured time, build cultural intelligence without stereotypes, and support non-native English speakers so everyone contributes.

Note one concrete phrase Neeley uses to invite a quiet team member to speak, and try it in your next call.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Cross cultural communication | Pellegrino Riccardi | TEDxBergen

A practical bridge from the whole block into one global room: state a purpose, handle questions, deliver problems cleanly, broker compromise, win buy-in, and close with decisions and owners — while slowing down and confirming what you heard across accents.

Tip: script a five-line run sheet for your next real global call — one line per skill — and read it aloud before you dial in.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 19 — Feedback & Performance Reviews

Series
Ted Lasso / The Office (US) — any scene of a manager giving a one-on-one performance review or honest feedback

Watch how the feedback lands — or doesn't. Notice whether the manager opens with real recognition, anchors praise in a result, names a clear development area, or just gives a vague "good job". Both the good and bad examples are useful.

Tip: pause and rewrite one vague line as STAR feedback — situation, action, result.

Disponível nos streamings: Apple TV+, Netflix, Max e Prime Video

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — The Secret to Giving Great Feedback

Kim Scott, author of Radical Candor, breaks down how to give honest feedback that is both specific and kind — caring personally while challenging directly, soliciting criticism, and offering praise that names a real result.

Listen for Scott's 'care personally, challenge directly' framework and script one balanced sentence using it.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
The secret to giving great feedback | The Way We Work, a TED series

A practical bridge from a fair review into the words that carry it: recognition with a result, an actionable development area, and reporting what was said accurately when you take it up the chain — "she said she was...", "he told me he would...".

Tip: after watching, report three things from the video using the backshift — "the speaker said that...".

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 20 — Coaching & Development Conversations

Series
Ted Lasso — any scene where Ted or Coach Beard draws someone out with questions instead of orders

A masterclass in coaching by asking, not telling. Watch how the best moments turn on a single open question that makes a player find their own answer — and how giving the answer would have shut it down.

Tip: pause after each closed question you hear and rewrite it as an open one (what / how).

Disponível no streaming: Apple TV+

Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — The Three Big Myths of Mentoring

Adam Grant digs into the science of good mentoring: what really builds a strong mentor-mentee bond, why the best mentors ask better questions and hand over ownership, and which common myths hold people back.

Listen for the open, coaching-style questions mentors use instead of just giving answers.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
The GROW Model for Coaching - Origins and application - Sir John Whitmore

A practical bridge into the two tools of this lesson: the open questions that make people think, and the zero conditional (if + present, present) that maps a growth plan as a system — "if you protect your mornings, the deep work gets done".

Tip: after watching, write three zero conditionals for your own development plan this quarter.

Assistir no YouTube ↗
